title: Russian literature

infobox.value: is the literature of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), its [émigrés](/wiki/Russian_diaspora "Russian diaspora"), and [Russian-language](/wiki/Russian-language "Russian-language") literature.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian1998vii-1) Major contributors to Russian literature, as well as English for instance, are authors of different ethnic origins, including bilingual writers, such as Kyrgyz novelist [Chinghiz Aitmatov](/wiki/Chinghiz_Aitmatov "Chinghiz Aitmatov") and [Vasil Bykaŭ](/wiki/Vasil_Byka%C5%AD "Vasil Bykaŭ"),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian1998vii-1) the latter wrote in [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), but translated his works into Russian. At the same time, Russian-language literature does not include works by authors from the Russian Federation who write exclusively or primarily in the native languages of the indigenous non-Russian [ethnic groups in Russia](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Russia "Ethnic groups in Russia"), thus the famous Dagestani poet [Rasul Gamzatov](/wiki/Rasul_Gamzatov "Rasul Gamzatov") is omitted.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Tolstoy_and_chekhov.jpg/250px-Tolstoy_and_chekhov.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tolstoy_and_chekhov.jpg)

[Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov") and [Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy"), 1901

**Russian literature** is the literature of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), its [émigrés](/wiki/Russian_diaspora "Russian diaspora"), and [Russian-language](/wiki/Russian-language "Russian-language") literature.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian1998vii-1) Major contributors to Russian literature, as well as English for instance, are authors of different ethnic origins, including bilingual writers, such as Kyrgyz novelist [Chinghiz Aitmatov](/wiki/Chinghiz_Aitmatov "Chinghiz Aitmatov") and [Vasil Bykaŭ](/wiki/Vasil_Byka%C5%AD "Vasil Bykaŭ"),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian1998vii-1) the latter wrote in [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), but translated his works into Russian. At the same time, Russian-language literature does not include works by authors from the Russian Federation who write exclusively or primarily in the native languages of the indigenous non-Russian [ethnic groups in Russia](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Russia "Ethnic groups in Russia"), thus the famous Dagestani poet [Rasul Gamzatov](/wiki/Rasul_Gamzatov "Rasul Gamzatov") is omitted.

The roots of Russian written literature can be traced to the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") when [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") was introduced as a [liturgical language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language") and became used as a [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language"). The native Russian [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") remained the use within [oral literature](/wiki/Oral_literature "Oral literature") as well as written for decrees, laws, messages, chronicles, military tales, and so on. By the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), literature had grown in importance, and from the early 1830s, Russian literature underwent an astounding "Golden Age" in poetry, prose and drama. The [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_literature "Romantic literature") movement contributed to a flowering of literary talent: poet [Vasily Zhukovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Zhukovsky "Vasily Zhukovsky") and later his protégé [Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin") came to the fore. [Mikhail Lermontov](/wiki/Mikhail_Lermontov "Mikhail Lermontov") was one of the most important poets and novelists. [Nikolai Gogol](/wiki/Nikolai_Gogol "Nikolai Gogol") and [Ivan Turgenev](/wiki/Ivan_Turgenev "Ivan Turgenev") wrote masterful short stories and novels. [Fyodor Dostoevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoevsky "Fyodor Dostoevsky") and [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy") became internationally renowned. Other important figures were [Ivan Goncharov](/wiki/Ivan_Goncharov "Ivan Goncharov"), [Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin](/wiki/Mikhail_Saltykov-Shchedrin "Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin") and [Nikolai Leskov](/wiki/Nikolai_Leskov "Nikolai Leskov"). In the second half of the century [Anton Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov") excelled in short stories and became a leading dramatist. The end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century is sometimes called the [Silver Age of Russian poetry](/wiki/Silver_Age_of_Russian_poetry "Silver Age of Russian poetry"). The poets most often associated with the "Silver Age" are [Konstantin Balmont](/wiki/Konstantin_Balmont "Konstantin Balmont"), [Valery Bryusov](/wiki/Valery_Bryusov "Valery Bryusov"), [Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok"), [Anna Akhmatova](/wiki/Anna_Akhmatova "Anna Akhmatova"), [Nikolay Gumilyov](/wiki/Nikolay_Gumilyov "Nikolay Gumilyov"), [Sergei Yesenin](/wiki/Sergei_Yesenin "Sergei Yesenin"), [Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky"), and [Marina Tsvetaeva](/wiki/Marina_Tsvetaeva "Marina Tsvetaeva"). This era produced novelists and short-story writers, such as [Aleksandr Kuprin](/wiki/Aleksandr_Kuprin "Aleksandr Kuprin"), Nobel Prize winner [Ivan Bunin](/wiki/Ivan_Bunin "Ivan Bunin"), [Leonid Andreyev](/wiki/Leonid_Andreyev "Leonid Andreyev"), [Fyodor Sologub](/wiki/Fyodor_Sologub "Fyodor Sologub"), [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin"), [Alexander Belyaev](/wiki/Alexander_Belyaev "Alexander Belyaev"), [Andrei Bely](/wiki/Andrei_Bely "Andrei Bely") and [Maxim Gorky](/wiki/Maxim_Gorky "Maxim Gorky").

After the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") of 1917, literature split into Soviet and [white émigré](/wiki/White_%C3%A9migr%C3%A9 "White émigré") parts. While the Soviet Union assured [universal literacy](/wiki/Likbez "Likbez") and a highly developed book printing industry, it also [established ideological censorship](/wiki/Censorship_in_the_Soviet_Union "Censorship in the Soviet Union"). In the 1930s [Socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism") became the predominant trend in Russia. Its leading figures were [Nikolay Ostrovsky](/wiki/Nikolay_Ostrovsky "Nikolay Ostrovsky"), [Alexander Fadeyev](/wiki/Alexander_Fadeyev_(writer) "Alexander Fadeyev (writer)") and other writers, who laid the foundations of this style. Ostrovsky's novel *[How the Steel Was Tempered](/wiki/How_the_Steel_Was_Tempered "How the Steel Was Tempered")* has been among the most popular works of Russian Socrealist literature. Some writers, such as [Mikhail Bulgakov](/wiki/Mikhail_Bulgakov "Mikhail Bulgakov"), [Andrei Platonov](/wiki/Andrei_Platonov "Andrei Platonov") and [Daniil Kharms](/wiki/Daniil_Kharms "Daniil Kharms") were criticized and wrote with little or no hope of being published. Various *émigré* writers, such as poets [Vladislav Khodasevich](/wiki/Vladislav_Khodasevich "Vladislav Khodasevich"), [Georgy Ivanov](/wiki/Georgy_Ivanov "Georgy Ivanov") and [Vyacheslav Ivanov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Ivanov_(poet) "Vyacheslav Ivanov (poet)"); novelists such as [Ivan Shmelyov](/wiki/Ivan_Shmelyov "Ivan Shmelyov"), [Gaito Gazdanov](/wiki/Gaito_Gazdanov "Gaito Gazdanov"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov") and Bunin, continued to write in exile. Some writers dared to oppose Soviet ideology, like Nobel Prize-winning novelist [Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn") and [Varlam Shalamov](/wiki/Varlam_Shalamov "Varlam Shalamov"), who wrote about life in the gulag camps. The [Khrushchev Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw") brought some fresh wind to literature and poetry became a mass cultural phenomenon. This "thaw" did not last long; in the 1970s, some of the most prominent authors were banned from publishing and prosecuted for their anti-Soviet sentiments.

The post-Soviet end of the 20th century was a difficult period for Russian literature, with few distinct voices. Among the most discussed authors of this period were novelists [Victor Pelevin](/wiki/Victor_Pelevin "Victor Pelevin") and [Vladimir Sorokin](/wiki/Vladimir_Sorokin "Vladimir Sorokin"), and the poet [Dmitri Prigov](/wiki/Dmitri_Prigov "Dmitri Prigov"). In the 21st century, a new generation of Russian authors appeared, differing greatly from the [postmodernist Russian](/wiki/Russian_postmodernism "Russian postmodernism") prose of the late 20th century, which led critics to speak about "new realism". Russian authors have significantly contributed to numerous literary genres. Russia has five [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") laureates. As of 2011, Russia was the [fourth largest book producer in the world](/wiki/Books_published_per_country_per_year "Books published per country per year") in terms of published titles.[[2]](#cite_note-2) A popular folk saying claims Russians are "the world's most reading nation".[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) As the American scholar [Gary Saul Morson](/wiki/Gary_Saul_Morson "Gary Saul Morson") notes, "No country has ever valued literature more than Russia."[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorson202341-5)

## Old oral literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Old oral literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD.jpg/250px-%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD.jpg)

The bard [Boyan](/wiki/Boyan_(bard) "Boyan (bard)") accompanying on the *gusli* by [Viktor Vasnetsov](/wiki/Viktor_Vasnetsov "Viktor Vasnetsov"), 1910

See also: [Russian folklore](/wiki/Russian_folklore "Russian folklore")

[Oral literature](/wiki/Oral_literature "Oral literature"), including [pagan myths](/wiki/Slavic_paganism "Slavic paganism") and prayers, ceremonial poetry, *[zagovory](/wiki/Zagovory "Zagovory")* (evocations), [charms](/wiki/Incantation "Incantation"), *[byliny](/wiki/Bylina "Bylina")* (heroic epics), [legends](/wiki/Legend "Legend"), [fairy tales](/wiki/Fairy_tale "Fairy tale"), folksongs, and others, in the languages of the [East Slavic](/wiki/East_Slavs "East Slavs") tribes, and later in the Old Russian [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") and its [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect"), is by definition older than written literature and accompanied the latter throughout its development.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlexander1973-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian19983–4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolov1950179,_203,_224,_235,_241,_291,_342,_371,_381-8)

> Заслышал Скимен-зверь невзгодушку:  
> Уж как на небе родился светел месяц,  
> На земле-то народился могуч богатырь.

—From the *[bylina](/wiki/Bylina "Bylina")* called *The Birth of a Knight-errant* ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Рождение богатыря).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Professional [storytelling](/wiki/Storytelling "Storytelling") existed, with bards of *[druzhiny](/wiki/Druzhina "Druzhina")* like [Boyan](/wiki/Boyan_(bard) "Boyan (bard)") playing the *[gusli](/wiki/Gusli "Gusli")* and wandering *[skomorokhi](/wiki/Skomorokh "Skomorokh")*. For a long time, folk literature was not recorded by scribes during the Kievan and Muscovite periods due to its pagan nature and, therefore, was largely lost. In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), folklore was widespread among all social strata, from peasants to the warriors and the *[knyaz](/wiki/Knyaz "Knyaz")*-*[boyar](/wiki/Boyar "Boyar")* aristocracy. With the advent of the written era, oral and written literature, with their own system of genres, coexisted in parallel. They complemented each other and sometimes came into close contact. Early examples of such reflection include the *Legend of the Founding of Kiev* and the *Legend of the brothers Radim and Vyatko*, the progenitors of the [Radimichs](/wiki/Radimichs "Radimichs") and [Vyatichi](/wiki/Vyatichi "Vyatichi") tribes, respectively, which were incorporated into medieval Russian [chronicles](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle") after being reworked.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolov1950-10)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] A later example is *[The Tale of Peter and Fevronia of Murom](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Peter_and_Fevronia "The Tale of Peter and Fevronia")*, which draws on local legends and fantastic tales.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolov1950284,_382-11)

## Medieval and early modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Medieval and early modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Novgorod_Codex_-_colour.jpg/250px-Novgorod_Codex_-_colour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Novgorod_Codex_-_colour.jpg)

First page of the [Novgorod Psalter](/wiki/Novgorod_Codex "Novgorod Codex") of c. 1000, the oldest survived Slavic book

Scholars typically use the term *Old Russian literature*, in addition to the terms *medieval Russian literature* and *early modern Russian literature*,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler201813-12) or *pre-Petrian literature*,[[13]](#cite_note-Morson-13) to refer to Russian written literature until the reforms of [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great"), tying literary development to historical periodization. The term is generally used to refer to all forms of literary activity in what is often called *Old Russia* from the 11th to 17th centuries.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwell200113-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985316-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Birch_bark_letter_N_955.jpg/250px-Birch_bark_letter_N_955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birch_bark_letter_N_955.jpg)

Personal correspondence, the [birch bark letter](/wiki/Birch_bark_manuscript "Birch bark manuscript") from Matchmaker's Milusha to Marena, 12th century, [Veliky Novgorod](/wiki/Veliky_Novgorod "Veliky Novgorod")

Literary works from this period were often written in the Russian recension of [Church Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic "Church Slavonic") with varying amounts of the Russian or more broadly [East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic") vernacular.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian19985-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2013128–132-17) At the same time, the native Old Russian vernacular was not only language of oral literature, such as epic poems (*[byliny](/wiki/Bylina "Bylina")*) or folksongs,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian19983–4-7) but it was also perfectly legitimate as written for practical purposes, such as decrees, laws (the *[Russkaya Pravda](/wiki/Russkaya_Pravda "Russkaya Pravda")*, the 11th–12th century, and other codes), letters (for example, the unique pre-paper [birch bark manuscripts](/wiki/Birch_bark_manuscript "Birch bark manuscript") of the 11th–15th centuries in the [Old Novgorod dialect](/wiki/Old_Novgorod_dialect "Old Novgorod dialect")), ambassadorial messages,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian19985-16) "in chronicles or military tales whose language is fundamentally the Russian vernacular."[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian19985-16)

Old Russian "bookish" literature traces its beginnings to the introduction of [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") in [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") as a liturgical language in the late 10th century following [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianization of Kievan Rus'").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser19923"The_Literature_of_Old_Russia,_988–1730"-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler201820–21-19) The [East Slavs](/wiki/East_Slavs "East Slavs") soon developed their own literature, and the oldest dated manuscript of Early Russian as well all-Slavic literature that has survived to this day is the [Novgorod Codex](/wiki/Novgorod_Codex "Novgorod Codex") or Novgorod Psalter written c. 1000, unearthed in 2000 at [Veliky Novgorod](/wiki/Veliky_Novgorod "Veliky Novgorod"), containing four wooden tablet pages filled with wax.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaliznyakYanin2001-20) Another earliest Russian book is the [Ostromir Gospels](/wiki/Ostromir_Gospels "Ostromir Gospels") written in 1056–1057, which belongs to the set of liturgical texts that were translated from other languages.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser19923–4-21)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVinokur197134-22)

> The discord of the princes ruined them against the Pagans. For, brother spake to brother;—"This is mine, and that is also mine." And the princes began to pronounce of a paltry thing, 'this is great'; and themselves amongst them to forge feuds; and the heathens from all sides advanced with victories against the Russian land.

—*[The Tale of Igor's Campaign](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Igor%27s_Campaign "The Tale of Igor's Campaign")*, 2.1 (c. 1185), translated by Leonard A. Magnus.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The main type of Old Russian historical literature were [chronicles](/wiki/Rus%27_chronicles "Rus' chronicles"), most of them anonymous.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler201827–29-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenkovsky1963-25) The oldest one is the *[Primary Chronicle](/wiki/Primary_Chronicle "Primary Chronicle")* or *Tale* of [Nestor the Chronicler](/wiki/Nestor_the_Chronicler "Nestor the Chronicler") (c. 1115).[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler201828-26)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenkovsky1963-25)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) The oldest surviving manuscripts include the [Laurentian Codex](/wiki/Laurentian_Codex "Laurentian Codex") of 1377 and the [Hypatian Codex](/wiki/Hypatian_Codex "Hypatian Codex") dating to the 1420s.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199212-28) Anonymous works include *[The Tale of Igor's Campaign](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Igor%27s_Campaign "The Tale of Igor's Campaign")* (a 12th century [prose poem](/wiki/Prose_poem "Prose poem") masterpiece)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenkovsky1963-25)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) and *[Praying of Daniel the Immured](/wiki/Praying_of_Daniel_the_Immured "Praying of Daniel the Immured")*.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Hagiographies ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): жития святых, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *zhitiya svyatykh*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'lives of the saints') formed a popular [literary genre](/wiki/Literary_genre "Literary genre") in Old Russian literature. The first notable hagiographer was Nestor the Chronicler, who wrote about the lives of [Boris and Gleb](/wiki/Boris_and_Gleb "Boris and Gleb"), the first saints of Kievan Rus', and the abbot [Theodosius](/wiki/Theodosius_of_Kiev "Theodosius of Kiev").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser19929-30) The *[Life of Alexander Nevsky](/wiki/Life_of_Alexander_Nevsky "Life of Alexander Nevsky")* is a well-known example, which combines political realism and hagiographical ideals, and concentrates on the key events of [Alexander Nevsky](/wiki/Alexander_Nevsky "Alexander Nevsky")'s political career.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199222-31) The earliest account of a pilgrimage is *The Pilgrimage of the Abbot Daniel*, which records the journey of [Daniel the Traveller](/wiki/Daniel_the_Traveller "Daniel the Traveller") to the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199211-32) Complex epic works such as *[The Tale of the Destruction of Ryazan](/wiki/The_Tale_of_the_Destruction_of_Ryazan "The Tale of the Destruction of Ryazan")* recall the havoc caused by the Mongol invasions.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199223-33) Other notable Russian literary works include *[Zadonschina](/wiki/Zadonschina "Zadonschina")*,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) *[Physiologist](/wiki/Physiologist_(Russian_literature) "Physiologist (Russian literature)")*, *[Synopsis](/wiki/Synopsis_(Russian_literature) "Synopsis (Russian literature)")* and *[A Journey Beyond the Three Seas](/wiki/A_Journey_Beyond_the_Three_Seas "A Journey Beyond the Three Seas")*.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser19921–44"The_Literature_of_Old_Russia,_988–1730"-34) Medieval Russian literature had an overwhelmingly religious character and used an adapted form of the Church Slavonic language with many South Slavic elements.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser19921–44-35)

In the 16th century, reflecting the political centralization and unification of the country under the [tsar](/wiki/Tsar "Tsar"), chronicles were updated and codified, the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") began issuing its decrees in the *[Stoglav](/wiki/Stoglav "Stoglav")*, and a large compilation called the *[Great Menaion Reader](/wiki/Great_Menaion_Reader "Great Menaion Reader")* collected both the more modern polemical texts and the hagiographical and patristic legacy of Old Russia.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199230–31-36) The *[Book of Royal Degrees](/wiki/Book_of_Royal_Degrees "Book of Royal Degrees")* codified the cult of the tsar, the *[Domostroy](/wiki/Domostroy "Domostroy")* laid down the rules for family life, and other texts such as the *[History of Kazan](/wiki/Kazan_Chronicle "Kazan Chronicle")* were used to justify the actions of the tsar.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199231-37) *[The Tale of Peter and Fevronia](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Peter_and_Fevronia "The Tale of Peter and Fevronia")* were among the original tales of this period, and Russian tsar [Ivan IV](/wiki/Ivan_the_Terrible "Ivan the Terrible") wrote some of most original works of 16th-century Russian literature.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199231-37) The [Time of Troubles](/wiki/Time_of_Troubles "Time of Troubles") marked a turning point in Old Russian literature as both the church and state lost control over the written word, which are reflected in the texts of writers such as [Avraamy Palitsyn](/wiki/Avraamy_Palitsyn "Avraamy Palitsyn") who developed a literary technique for representing complex characters.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199233-38)

In the second half of the 17th century, the literature of [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") took shape, primarily due to the initiative of tsar [Alexis of Russia](/wiki/Alexis_of_Russia "Alexis of Russia"), who wanted to open a court theatre in 1672. Its director and playwright was Johann Gottfried Gregorii, a German-Russian pastor, who wrote, in particular, the 10-hour [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)") *The Action of Artaxerxes*. The poetry and dramaturgy of [Symeon of Polotsk](/wiki/Symeon_of_Polotsk "Symeon of Polotsk") and [Demetrius of Rostov](/wiki/Demetrius_of_Rostov "Demetrius of Rostov") contributed to the development of the Russian version of the Baroque.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018153–157-39)

In the 17th century, when bookmen from the [Kiev Academy](/wiki/Kiev_Academy "Kiev Academy") arrived in Moscow, they brought with them a
culture heavily influenced by the educational system of the Polish [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199234-40) Mentioned Symeon of Polotsk created a new style which fused elements of ancient and contemporary Western European literature with traditional Russian rhetoric and the imperial ideology, which marked a key step in the Westernization of Russian literature.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199235-41) [Syllabic poetry](/wiki/Syllabic_poetry "Syllabic poetry") was also brought to Russia, and the work of Simeon of Polotsk was continued by [Sylvester Medvedev](/wiki/Sylvester_Medvedev "Sylvester Medvedev") and [Karion Istomin](/wiki/Karion_Istomin "Karion Istomin").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199235-41)

> "Will these sufferings go on a long time, Archpriest?" And I said, "Markovna, right up to our very death." And so she sighed and answered, "Good enough, Petrovič, then let's be getting on."

—[Avvakum](/wiki/Avvakum "Avvakum"), *The Life written by Himself* (1672), translated by Kenneth N. Brostrom[[42]](#cite_note-42)

*The Life of the Archpriest Avvakum*—an outstanding novelty [autobiography](/wiki/Autobiography "Autobiography") written by the one of leaders of the 17th-century religious dissidents [Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers") [Avvakum](/wiki/Avvakum "Avvakum")—is considered masterpiece of pre-Petrian literature, which blends high Old Church Slavonic with low Russian vernacular and profanity without following literary canons.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018142–145-43)

## Age of Enlightenment, 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Age of Enlightenment, 18th century")]

After taking the throne at the end of the 17th century, [Peter the Great's](/wiki/Peter_I_of_Russia "Peter I of Russia") influence on the Russian culture would extend far into the [18th century](/wiki/18th_century_in_literature "18th century in literature"). Peter's reign during the beginning of the 18th century initiated a series of modernizing changes in Russian literature. The reforms he implemented encouraged Russian artists and scientists to make innovations in their crafts and fields with the intention of creating an economy and culture comparable. Peter's example set a precedent for the remainder of the 18th century as Russian writers began to form clear ideas about the proper use and progression of the Russian language. Through their debates regarding versification of the Russian language and tone of Russian literature, the writers in the first half of the 18th century were able to lay foundation for the more poignant, topical work of the late 18th century.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199245–91"The_Eighteenth_Century:_Neoclassicism_and_the_Enlightenment,_1730–1790"-44)

[Satirist](/wiki/Satire "Satire") [Antiokh Dmitrievich Kantemir](/wiki/Antiokh_Dmitrievich_Kantemir "Antiokh Dmitrievich Kantemir"), 1708–1744, was one of the earliest Russian writers not only to praise the ideals of Peter I's reforms but the ideals of the growing [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") movement in Europe. Kantemir's works regularly expressed his admiration for Peter, most notably in his epic dedicated to the emperor entitled *Petrida*. More often, however, Kantemir indirectly praised Peter's influence through his satiric criticism of Russia's "superficiality and obscurantism", which he saw as manifestations of the backwardness Peter attempted to correct through his reforms.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985221–223-45) Kantemir honored this tradition of reform not only through his support for Peter, but by initiating a decade-long debate on the proper syllabic versification using the Russian language.

[Vasily Kirillovich Trediakovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Kirillovich_Trediakovsky "Vasily Kirillovich Trediakovsky"), a poet, playwright, essayist, translator and contemporary to Antiokh Kantemir, also found himself deeply entrenched in Enlightenment conventions in his work with the [Russian Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Russian Academy of Sciences") and his groundbreaking translations of French and classical works to the Russian language. A turning point in the course of Russian literature, his translation of [Paul Tallemant's](/wiki/Paul_Tallement_the_Younger "Paul Tallement the Younger") work *Voyage to the Isle of Love*, was the first to use the Russian vernacular as opposed the formal and outdated [Church-Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic_language "Church Slavonic language").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985474–477-46) This introduction set a precedent for secular works to be composed in the vernacular, while sacred texts would remain in Church-Slavonic. However, his work was often incredibly theoretical and scholarly, focused on promoting the versification of the language with which he spoke.

While Trediakovsky's approach to writing is often described as highly erudite, the young writer and scholarly rival to Trediakovsky, [Alexander Petrovich Sumarokov](/wiki/Alexander_Sumarokov "Alexander Sumarokov"), 1717–1777, was dedicated to the styles of French [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199245–91"The_Eighteenth_Century:_Neoclassicism_and_the_Enlightenment,_1730–1790"-44) Sumarokov's interest in the form of the [17th-century French literature](/wiki/17th-century_French_literature "17th-century French literature") mirrored his devotion to the westernizing spirit of Peter the Great's age. Although he often disagreed with Trediakovsky, Sumarokov also advocated the use of simple, natural language in order to diversify the audience and make more efficient use of the Russian language. Like his colleagues and counterparts, Sumarokov extolled the legacy of Peter I, writing in his manifesto *Epistle on Poetry*, "The great Peter hurls his thunder from the Baltic shores, the Russian sword glitters in all corners of the universe".[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELang1948502-47) Peter the Great's policies of westernization and displays of military prowess naturally attracted Sumarokov and his contemporaries.

[Mikhail Vasilyevich Lomonosov](/wiki/Mikhail_Vasilyevich_Lomonosov "Mikhail Vasilyevich Lomonosov"), in particular, expressed his gratitude for and dedication to Peter's legacy in his unfinished *Peter the Great*, Lomonosov's works often focused on themes of the awe-inspiring, grandeur nature, and was therefore drawn to Peter because of the magnitude of his military, architectural and cultural feats. In contrast to Sumarokov's devotion to simplicity, Lomonosov favored a belief in a hierarchy of literary styles divided into high, middle and low. This style facilitated Lomonosov's grandiose, high minded writing and use of both vernacular and Church-Slavonic.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELang1948500-48)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199245–91"The_Eighteenth_Century:_Neoclassicism_and_the_Enlightenment,_1730–1790"-44)

The influence of Peter I and debates over the function and form of literature as it related to the Russian language in the first half of the 18th century set a stylistic precedent for the writers during the reign of [Catherine the Great](/wiki/Catherine_the_Great "Catherine the Great") in the second half of the century. However, the themes and scopes of the works these writers produced were often more poignant, political and controversial. [Ippolit Bogdanovich](/wiki/Ippolit_Bogdanovich "Ippolit Bogdanovich")'s narrative poem *Dushenka* (1778) is rare sample of the [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") style, erotic [light poetry](/wiki/Light_poetry "Light poetry") in Russia.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199283-49) [Alexander Nikolayevich Radishchev](/wiki/Alexander_Radishchev "Alexander Radishchev"), for example, shocked the Russian public with his depictions of the socio-economic condition of the [serfs](/wiki/Serfs "Serfs"). Empress Catherine II condemned this portrayal, forcing Radishchev into exile in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985365–366-50)

Others, however, picked topics less offensive to the [autocrat](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy"). the historian and writer [Nikolay Karamzin](/wiki/Nikolay_Karamzin "Nikolay Karamzin"), 1766–1826, the key figure of [literary sentimentalism](/wiki/Sentimentalism_(literature) "Sentimentalism (literature)") in Russia,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[13]](#cite_note-Morson-13)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser199292–135"The_Transition_to_the_Modern_Age:_Sentimentalism_and_Preromanticism,_1790–1820"-51) for example, is known for his advocacy of Russian writers adopting traits in the poetry and prose like a heightened sense of emotion and physical vanity, considered to be feminine at the time as well as supporting the cause of female Russian writers.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenholmSavkina2012162–163-52)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwellChristian199835–40-53)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwell2001150–162-54) Karamzin's call for male writers to write with femininity was not in accordance with the Enlightenment ideals of reason and theory, considered masculine attributes. His works were thus not universally well received; however, they did reflect in some areas of society a growing respect for, or at least ambivalence toward, a female ruler in Catherine the Great. This concept heralded an era of regarding female characteristics in writing as an abstract concept linked with attributes of frivolity, vanity and pathos.

Some writers, on the other hand, were more direct in their praise for Catherine II. [Gavrila Romanovich Derzhavin](/wiki/Gavrila_Romanovich_Derzhavin "Gavrila Romanovich Derzhavin"), famous for his odes, often dedicated his poems to Empress Catherine II. In contrast to most of his contemporaries, Derzhavin was highly devoted to his state; he served in the military, before rising to various roles in Catherine II's government, including secretary to the Empress and Minister of Justice. Unlike those who took after the grand style of Mikhail Lomonosov and Alexander Sumarokov, Derzhavin was concerned with the minute details of his subjects.

[Denis Fonvizin](/wiki/Denis_Fonvizin "Denis Fonvizin"), an author primarily of comedy, approached the subject of the [Russian nobility](/wiki/Russian_nobility "Russian nobility") with an angle of critique. Fonvizin felt the nobility should be held to the standards they were under the reign of Peter the Great, during which the quality of devotion to the state was rewarded. His works criticized the current system for rewarding the nobility without holding them responsible for the duties they once performed. Using satire and comedy, Fonvizin supported a system of nobility in which the elite were rewarded based upon personal merit rather than the hierarchal favoritism that was rampant during Catherine the Great's reign.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Golden Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Golden Age")]

See also: [Romanticism § Russia](/wiki/Romanticism#Russia "Romanticism"), and [List of romantics § Russian Romanticism](/wiki/List_of_romantics#Russian_Romanticism "List of romantics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Painted-portraits-of-writer.png/250px-Painted-portraits-of-writer.png)](/wiki/File:Painted-portraits-of-writer.png)

[Krylov](/wiki/Ivan_Krylov "Ivan Krylov"), [Pushkin](/wiki/Pushkin "Pushkin"), [Zhukovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Zhukovsky "Vasily Zhukovsky"), and [Gnedich](/wiki/Nikolay_Gnedich "Nikolay Gnedich") in the [Summer Garden](/wiki/Summer_Garden "Summer Garden") by [Grigory Chernetsov](/wiki/Grigory_Chernetsov "Grigory Chernetsov") (1832)

> I lay, and heard the voice of God:  
> "Arise, oh prophet, watch and hearken,  
> And with my Will thy soul engird,  
> Through lands that dim and seas that darken,  
> Burn thou men's hearts with this, my Word."

[Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin"), *The Prophet* (1826), translated by   
 [Babette Deutsch](/wiki/Babette_Deutsch "Babette Deutsch") and [Avrahm Yarmolinsky](/wiki/Avrahm_Yarmolinsky "Avrahm Yarmolinsky")[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The [19th century](/wiki/19th_century_in_literature "19th century in literature") is traditionally referred to as the "Golden Era" of Russian literature.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1950-57)
The period of [Romantic literature](/wiki/Romantic_literature "Romantic literature") saw the flowering of poetic talent, in particular; the names of [Vasily Zhukovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Zhukovsky "Vasily Zhukovsky") and his protégé [Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin") came to the fore.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser1992136–188"The_Nineteenth_Century:_Romanticism,_1820–1840"-58) Pushkin is credited with crystallizing the literary Russian language and introducing a new level of artistry to Russian literature. His best-known work is a pre-realistic novel in verse, *[Eugene Onegin](/wiki/Eugene_Onegin "Eugene Onegin")* (1833).[[59]](#cite_note-59) Other poets important to the movement include [Konstantin Batyushkov](/wiki/Konstantin_Batyushkov "Konstantin Batyushkov"), [Pyotr Vyazemsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Vyazemsky "Pyotr Vyazemsky"), [Yevgeny Baratynsky](/wiki/Yevgeny_Baratynsky "Yevgeny Baratynsky"), [Fyodor Tyutchev](/wiki/Fyodor_Tyutchev "Fyodor Tyutchev") and [Dmitry Venevitinov](/wiki/Dmitry_Venevitinov "Dmitry Venevitinov"), along with the novelists [Antony Pogorelsky](/wiki/Antony_Pogorelsky "Antony Pogorelsky"), [Alexander Bestuzhev](/wiki/Alexander_Bestuzhev "Alexander Bestuzhev") and "Russian Hoffmann" [Vladimir Odoyevsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Odoyevsky "Vladimir Odoyevsky"). Tyutchev is best known for the following verse:

> *Who would grasp Russia with the mind?*  
> *For her no yardstick was created:*  
> *Her soul is of a special kind,*  
> *By faith alone appreciated.*

— [Fyodor Tyutchev](/wiki/Fyodor_Tyutchev "Fyodor Tyutchev"), *Who would grasp Russia with the mind?* (1866), translated by John Dewey[[60]](#cite_note-60)

An entire new generation of Romantic poets and novelists followed in Pushkin's steps. [Mikhail Lermontov](/wiki/Mikhail_Lermontov "Mikhail Lermontov") wrote the narrative poem *[Demon](/wiki/Demon_(poem) "Demon (poem)")* in 1829–39, which chronicled the love of a Byronic Demon for a mortal woman, as well as *[A Hero of Our Time](/wiki/A_Hero_of_Our_Time "A Hero of Our Time")* (1841), which is often considered to be the first Russian [psychological novel](/wiki/Psychological_fiction "Psychological fiction"). [Aleksey K. Tolstoy](/wiki/Aleksey_Konstantinovich_Tolstoy "Aleksey Konstantinovich Tolstoy") and [Afanasy Fet](/wiki/Afanasy_Fet "Afanasy Fet") were also significant.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser1992136–188"The_Nineteenth_Century:_Romanticism,_1820–1840"-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Russian_writers_by_Levitsky_1856.jpg/250px-Russian_writers_by_Levitsky_1856.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russian_writers_by_Levitsky_1856.jpg)

The group picture of Russian writers, the literary magazine *[Sovremennik](/wiki/Sovremennik "Sovremennik")* editorial board members. [Ivan Goncharov](/wiki/Ivan_Goncharov "Ivan Goncharov"), [Ivan Turgenev](/wiki/Ivan_Turgenev "Ivan Turgenev"), [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy"), [Dmitry Grigorovich](/wiki/Dmitry_Grigorovich_(writer) "Dmitry Grigorovich (writer)"), [Alexander Druzhinin](/wiki/Alexander_Druzhinin "Alexander Druzhinin"), and [Aleksandr Ostrovsky](/wiki/Aleksandr_Ostrovsky "Aleksandr Ostrovsky"), 1856

As Romanticism came to command the stage, the Age of [Realism](/wiki/Literary_realism "Literary realism")[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) began to flourish as well. The first great Russian rich language novel was *[Dead Souls](/wiki/Dead_Souls "Dead Souls")* (1842) by [Nikolai Gogol](/wiki/Nikolai_Gogol "Nikolai Gogol").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrakhovsky1953-61) The realistic [natural school](/wiki/Natural_school "Natural school") of fiction is said to have begun with the works of [Ivan Goncharov](/wiki/Ivan_Goncharov "Ivan Goncharov"), mainly remembered for his novel *[Oblomov](/wiki/Oblomov "Oblomov")* (1859), as well as [Ivan Turgenev](/wiki/Ivan_Turgenev "Ivan Turgenev").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser1992189–247"The_Nineteenth_Century:_The_Natural_School_and_Its_Aftermath,_1840–55"-62) [Fyodor Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoyevsky "Fyodor Dostoyevsky") and [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy") soon became internationally renowned,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) to the point that scholars, such as [F. R. Leavis](/wiki/F._R._Leavis "F. R. Leavis"), have frequently described them as among the greatest novelists of all time. Tolstoy's [Christian anarchism](/wiki/Christian_anarchism "Christian anarchism") can be seen in the following quote:

> Plants, birds, insects and children were equally joyful. Only men—grown-up men—continued cheating and tormenting themselves and each other. People saw nothing holy in this spring morning, in this beauty of God's world—a gift to all living creatures—inclining to peace, good-will and love, but worshiped their own inventions for imposing their will on each other.

— [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy"), [*The Resurrection*](/wiki/Resurrection_(Tolstoy_novel) "Resurrection (Tolstoy novel)"), 1.1 (1899), translated by William E. Smith[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin](/wiki/Mikhail_Saltykov-Shchedrin "Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin") is known for his satirical chronicle *[The History of a Town](/wiki/The_History_of_a_Town "The History of a Town")* (1870) and the family saga *[The Golovlyov Family](/wiki/The_Golovlyov_Family "The Golovlyov Family")* (1880), which are considered his masterpieces. [Nikolai Leskov](/wiki/Nikolai_Leskov "Nikolai Leskov") is best remembered for his shorter fiction and for his (together with [Pavel Melnikov](/wiki/Pavel_Ivanovich_Melnikov "Pavel Ivanovich Melnikov")) unique *[skaz](/wiki/Skaz "Skaz")* techniques, namely oral form of narrative stylization. Late in the century [Anton Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov") emerged as a master of the short story as well as a leading international dramatist.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)

Other important 19th-century developments included [Sergey Aksakov](/wiki/Sergey_Aksakov "Sergey Aksakov")'s semi-autobiographical writings; the father of Russian [social realism](/wiki/Social_realism "Social realism") and left-wing poetry school, known for the sharp epic poem *[Who Can Be Happy and Free in Russia?](/wiki/Who_Can_Be_Happy_and_Free_in_Russia%3F "Who Can Be Happy and Free in Russia?")* [Nikolay Nekrasov](/wiki/Nikolay_Nekrasov "Nikolay Nekrasov");[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) the fabulist [Ivan Krylov](/wiki/Ivan_Krylov "Ivan Krylov"); the precursor to [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") [Aleksey Pisemsky](/wiki/Aleksey_Pisemsky "Aleksey Pisemsky"); non-fiction writers such as the critic [Vissarion Belinsky](/wiki/Vissarion_Belinsky "Vissarion Belinsky") and the political reformer [Alexander Herzen](/wiki/Alexander_Herzen "Alexander Herzen"); playwrights such as [Aleksandr Griboyedov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Griboyedov "Aleksandr Griboyedov"), [Aleksandr Ostrovsky](/wiki/Aleksandr_Ostrovsky "Aleksandr Ostrovsky"),[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeasly1928-64) [Alexander Sukhovo-Kobylin](/wiki/Alexander_Sukhovo-Kobylin "Alexander Sukhovo-Kobylin") and the satirist [Kozma Prutkov](/wiki/Kozma_Prutkov "Kozma Prutkov") (a collective pen name).

## Silver Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Silver Age")]

Main article: [Silver Age of Russian poetry](/wiki/Silver_Age_of_Russian_poetry "Silver Age of Russian poetry")

> Night, street and streetlight, drug store,  
> The purposeless, half-dim, drab light.  
> For all the use live on a quarter century —  
> Nothing will change. There's no way out.  
>   
> You'll die — and start all over, live twice,  
> Everything repeats itself, just as it was:  
> Night, the canal's rippled icy surface,  
> The drug store, the street, and streetlight.

[Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok"), *Night, street and streetlight, drug store...* (1912), translated by Alex Cigale

The 1890s and the beginning of the 20th century ranks as the Silver Age of Russian poetry.[[13]](#cite_note-Morson-13) Well-known poets of the period include: [Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok"), [Sergei Yesenin](/wiki/Sergei_Yesenin "Sergei Yesenin"), [Valery Bryusov](/wiki/Valery_Bryusov "Valery Bryusov"), [Konstantin Balmont](/wiki/Konstantin_Balmont "Konstantin Balmont"), [Mikhail Kuzmin](/wiki/Mikhail_Kuzmin "Mikhail Kuzmin"), [Igor Severyanin](/wiki/Igor_Severyanin "Igor Severyanin"), [Sasha Chorny](/wiki/Sasha_Chorny "Sasha Chorny"), [Nikolay Gumilyov](/wiki/Nikolay_Gumilyov "Nikolay Gumilyov"), [Maximilian Voloshin](/wiki/Maximilian_Voloshin "Maximilian Voloshin"), [Innokenty Annensky](/wiki/Innokenty_Annensky "Innokenty Annensky"), [Zinaida Gippius](/wiki/Zinaida_Gippius "Zinaida Gippius"). The poets most often associated with the "Silver Age" are [Anna Akhmatova](/wiki/Anna_Akhmatova "Anna Akhmatova"), [Marina Tsvetaeva](/wiki/Marina_Tsvetaeva "Marina Tsvetaeva"), [Osip Mandelstam](/wiki/Osip_Mandelstam "Osip Mandelstam"), and [Boris Pasternak](/wiki/Boris_Pasternak "Boris Pasternak").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)

The [Russian symbolism](/wiki/Russian_symbolism "Russian symbolism") was the first Silver Age development in the 1890s. It arose enough separately from West European symbolism, emphasizing mysticism of [Sophiology](/wiki/Sophiology "Sophiology") and [defamiliarization](/wiki/Defamiliarization "Defamiliarization"). Its most significant figures included philosopher and poet [Vladimir Solovyov](/wiki/Vladimir_Solovyov_(philosopher) "Vladimir Solovyov (philosopher)") (1853–1900), poets and writers [Valery Bryusov](/wiki/Valery_Bryusov "Valery Bryusov") (1873–1924), [Fyodor Sologub](/wiki/Fyodor_Sologub "Fyodor Sologub") (1863–1927), [Vyacheslav Ivanov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Ivanov_(poet) "Vyacheslav Ivanov (poet)") (1866–1949), [Konstantin Balmont](/wiki/Konstantin_Balmont "Konstantin Balmont") (1867–1942), and figures of the new wave generation [Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok") (1880–1921) with [Andrei Bely](/wiki/Andrei_Bely "Andrei Bely") (1880–1934).[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeterson1993-66)[[13]](#cite_note-Morson-13)[[67]](#cite_note-symbolism-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Poshechina_obshestvennomu_vkusu.jpg/250px-Poshechina_obshestvennomu_vkusu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poshechina_obshestvennomu_vkusu.jpg)

Group photograph of some [Russian Futurists](/wiki/Russian_Futurism "Russian Futurism"), published in their manifesto *A Slap in the Face of Public Taste*. Left to right: [Aleksei Kruchyonykh](/wiki/Aleksei_Kruchyonykh "Aleksei Kruchyonykh"), [Vladimir Burliuk](/wiki/Vladimir_Burliuk "Vladimir Burliuk"), [Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky"), [David Burliuk](/wiki/David_Burliuk "David Burliuk"), and [Benedikt Livshits](/wiki/Benedikt_Livshits "Benedikt Livshits").

*[New peasant poets](/wiki/New_peasant_poets "New peasant poets")* was the conditional collective name of a group of peasant origin and country poetry trend ([Nikolai Klyuev](/wiki/Nikolai_Klyuev "Nikolai Klyuev"), Pyotr Oreshin, Alexander Shiryaevets, [Sergei Klychkov](/wiki/Sergei_Klychkov "Sergei Klychkov"), [Sergei Yesenin](/wiki/Sergei_Yesenin "Sergei Yesenin")). While the Silver Age is considered to be the development of the 19th-century Russian Golden Age literature tradition, some modernist and [avant-garde](/wiki/Russian_avant-garde "Russian avant-garde") poets tried to overturn it. Most prominent their movements: the [Cubo-Futurism](/wiki/Cubo-Futurism "Cubo-Futurism") with practice of *[zaum](/wiki/Zaum "Zaum")*, the experimental visual and sound poetry ([David Burliuk](/wiki/David_Burliuk "David Burliuk"), [Velimir Khlebnikov](/wiki/Velimir_Khlebnikov "Velimir Khlebnikov"), [Aleksei Kruchenykh](/wiki/Aleksei_Kruchenykh "Aleksei Kruchenykh"), [Nikolai Aseyev](/wiki/Nikolai_Aseyev_(writer) "Nikolai Aseyev (writer)"), [Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky"));[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985197-68) the [Ego-Futurism](/wiki/Ego-Futurism "Ego-Futurism") based on a personality cult ([Igor Severyanin](/wiki/Igor_Severyanin "Igor Severyanin") and [Vasilisk Gnedov](/wiki/Vasilisk_Gnedov "Vasilisk Gnedov"));[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkov196864-69) and the [Acmeist poetry](/wiki/Acmeist_poetry "Acmeist poetry"), a Russian modernist school, which emerged ca. 1911 and to symbols preferred direct expression through exact images ([Anna Akhmatova](/wiki/Anna_Akhmatova "Anna Akhmatova"), [Nikolay Gumilev](/wiki/Nikolay_Gumilev "Nikolay Gumilev"), [Georgiy Ivanov](/wiki/Georgiy_Ivanov "Georgiy Ivanov"), [Mikhail Kuzmin](/wiki/Mikhail_Kuzmin "Mikhail Kuzmin"), [Osip Mandelstam](/wiki/Osip_Mandelstam "Osip Mandelstam")).[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPainter20125–6-70)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWachtel20048-71)

Though the Silver Age is famous mostly for its poetry, it produced some first-rate novelists and short-story writers, such as [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") [Aleksandr Kuprin](/wiki/Aleksandr_Kuprin "Aleksandr Kuprin"), realists Nobel Prize winner [Ivan Bunin](/wiki/Ivan_Bunin "Ivan Bunin")[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) and [Vikenty Veresaev](/wiki/Vikenty_Veresaev "Vikenty Veresaev"), pioneer of Russian [expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism#Literature "Expressionism") [Leonid Andreyev](/wiki/Leonid_Andreyev "Leonid Andreyev"), symbolists [Fedor Sologub](/wiki/Fyodor_Sologub "Fyodor Sologub"), [Aleksey Remizov](/wiki/Aleksey_Remizov "Aleksey Remizov"), [Dmitry Merezhkovsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Merezhkovsky "Dmitry Merezhkovsky"), [Andrei Bely](/wiki/Andrei_Bely "Andrei Bely"), [Alexander Belyaev](/wiki/Alexander_Belyaev "Alexander Belyaev"), and [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin"), though most of them wrote poetry as well as prose.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)

In 1915/16, the school of [Russian Formalism](/wiki/Russian_Formalism "Russian Formalism"), wary of the futurists and highly influential for the global theory of literary criticism and [poetics](/wiki/Poetics "Poetics"), appeared; its programmatic article *The Resurrection of the Word* by the scholar and writer [Viktor Shklovsky](/wiki/Viktor_Shklovsky "Viktor Shklovsky") (1893–1984) was published in 1914, and the peak of activity occurred in the post-revolutionary 1920s.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteiner1984-72)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018532–534-73) An integral part of the literature of the Silver Age is [Russian philosophy](/wiki/Russian_philosophy "Russian philosophy"), which reached its peak at this time (see works of [Nikolai Berdyaev](/wiki/Nikolai_Berdyaev "Nikolai Berdyaev"), [Pavel Florensky](/wiki/Pavel_Florensky "Pavel Florensky"), [Semyon Frank](/wiki/Semyon_Frank "Semyon Frank"), [Nikolay Lossky](/wiki/Nikolay_Lossky "Nikolay Lossky"), [Vasily Rozanov](/wiki/Vasily_Rozanov "Vasily Rozanov"), and others).[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELossky1952-74)

## Soviet era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Soviet era")]

### Early post-Revolutionary era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early post-Revolutionary era")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Serapins.jpg/330px-Serapins.jpg)

The [Serapion Brothers](/wiki/Serapion_Brothers "Serapion Brothers") (use a cursor to see who is who)

> Tramp squares with rebellious treading!  
> Up heads! As proud peaks be seen!  
> In the second flood we are spreading  
> Every city on earth will be clean.'

[Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky"), *Our March* (1917), translation[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The first years of the Soviet regime after the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") of 1917, featured a proliferation of [Russian avant-garde](/wiki/Russian_avant-garde "Russian avant-garde") literary groups, and [proletarian literature](/wiki/Proletarian_literature "Proletarian literature") receive official support. The [Imaginists](/wiki/Imaginism "Imaginism") were post-Revolution poetic movement, similar to English-language [Imagists](/wiki/Imagism "Imagism"), that created poetry based on sequences of arresting and uncommon images. The major figures include [Sergei Yesenin](/wiki/Sergei_Yesenin "Sergei Yesenin"), [Anatoly Marienhof](/wiki/Anatoly_Marienhof "Anatoly Marienhof"), and [Rurik Ivnev](/wiki/Rurik_Ivnev "Rurik Ivnev").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENilsson1970-76) Another important movement was the [Oberiu](/wiki/Oberiu "Oberiu") (1927–1930s), which included the most famous Russian absurdist [Daniil Kharms](/wiki/Daniil_Kharms "Daniil Kharms") (1905–1942), [Konstantin Vaginov](/wiki/Konstantin_Vaginov "Konstantin Vaginov") (1899–1934), [Alexander Vvedensky](/wiki/Alexander_Vvedensky_(poet) "Alexander Vvedensky (poet)") (1904–1941) and [Nikolay Zabolotsky](/wiki/Nikolay_Zabolotsky "Nikolay Zabolotsky") (1903–1958).[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasack1988-77)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018621–624-78)

Other famous authors experimenting with language included the novelists [Boris Pilnyak](/wiki/Boris_Pilnyak "Boris Pilnyak") (1894–1938), [Yuri Olesha](/wiki/Yuri_Olesha "Yuri Olesha") (1899–1960), [Andrei Platonov](/wiki/Andrei_Platonov "Andrei Platonov") (1899–1951) and [Artyom Vesyoly](/wiki/Artyom_Vesyoly "Artyom Vesyoly") (1899–1938), the short-story writers [Isaak Babel](/wiki/Isaak_Babel "Isaak Babel") (1894–1940) and [Mikhail Zoshchenko](/wiki/Mikhail_Zoshchenko "Mikhail Zoshchenko") (1894–1958).[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasack1988-77) The [OPOJAZ](/wiki/OPOJAZ "OPOJAZ") group of literary critics, a part of [Russian formalism](/wiki/Russian_formalism "Russian formalism") school, was founded in 1916 in close connection with [Russian Futurism](/wiki/Russian_Futurism "Russian Futurism"). Two of its members also produced influential literary works, namely [Viktor Shklovsky](/wiki/Viktor_Shklovsky "Viktor Shklovsky"), whose numerous books (*A Sentimental Journey* and *Zoo, or Letters Not About Love*, both 1923) defy genre in that they present a novel mix of narration, autobiography, and aesthetic as well as social commentary, and [Yury Tynyanov](/wiki/Yury_Tynyanov "Yury Tynyanov") (1893–1943), who used his knowledge of Russia's literary history to produce a set of historical novels mainly set in the Pushkin era (e.g., *[Lieutenant Kijé](/wiki/Lieutenant_Kij%C3%A9 "Lieutenant Kijé")*, *Pushkin* in three parts, 1935–43, and others).[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteiner1984-72)

Following the establishment of [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") rule, [Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky") worked on interpreting the facts of the new reality. His works, such as "Ode to the Revolution" and "Left March" (both 1918), brought innovations to poetry. In "Left March", Mayakovsky calls for a struggle against the enemies of the Russian Revolution. The poem *[150 000 000](/wiki/150_000_000 "150 000 000")* (1921) discusses the leading role played by the masses in the revolution. In the poem *[Vladimir Ilyich Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Ilyich_Lenin_(poem) "Vladimir Ilyich Lenin (poem)")* (1924), Mayakovsky looks at the life and work at the leader of Russia's revolution and depicts them against a broad historical background. In the poem *[All Right!](/wiki/All_Right! "All Right!")* (1927), Mayakovsky writes about [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") society as the "springtime of humanity". Mayakovsky was instrumental in producing a new type of poetry in which politics played a major part.[[79]](#cite_note-79) One of the most popular Soviet poets during the 1920s was [Nikolai Tikhonov](/wiki/Nikolai_Tikhonov_(writer) "Nikolai Tikhonov (writer)") (1896–1979), a future important figure of Stalinist era, well-known for his *Ballad About Nails*,[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985474-80) as follows:

> Could nails from such people be fashioned, you’d see  
> That no tougher nails in the world would there be.

— [Nikolai Tikhonov](/wiki/Nikolai_Tikhonov_(writer) "Nikolai Tikhonov (writer)"), *Ballad of the Nails* (1919), translated by Peter Tempest[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### *Émigré* writers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Émigré writers")]

> I am an American writer, born in Russia, educated in England, where I studied French literature before moving to Germany for fifteen years. ... My head speaks English, my heart speaks Russian, and my ear speaks French.

[Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), from the interview

Usually, Russian *émigré* literature is understood as the works of the [white émigré](/wiki/White_%C3%A9migr%C3%A9 "White émigré"), namely the first post-Revolutionary wave, although in the broad sense of the word, it also includes [Soviet dissidents](/wiki/Soviet_dissidents "Soviet dissidents") of the late years through the 1980s.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018536–542-82) Meanwhile, émigré writers, such as poets [Georgy Ivanov](/wiki/Georgy_Ivanov "Georgy Ivanov"), [Vyacheslav Ivanov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Ivanov_(poet) "Vyacheslav Ivanov (poet)"), [Vladislav Khodasevich](/wiki/Vladislav_Khodasevich "Vladislav Khodasevich"), [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") Boris Poplavsky (1903–1935), and members of the 1920s–50s Paris Note (French: *Note parisienne*) Russian poetry movement ([Georgy Adamovich](/wiki/Georgy_Adamovich "Georgy Adamovich"), Igor Chinnov, [George Ivask](/wiki/George_Ivask "George Ivask"), [Anatoly Shteiger](/wiki/Anatoly_Shteiger "Anatoly Shteiger"), Lidia Tcherminskaia); novelists such as [M. Ageyev](/wiki/M._Ageyev "M. Ageyev"), [Mark Aldanov](/wiki/Mark_Aldanov "Mark Aldanov"), [Gaito Gazdanov](/wiki/Gaito_Gazdanov "Gaito Gazdanov"), [Pyotr Krasnov](/wiki/Pyotr_Krasnov "Pyotr Krasnov"), [Aleksandr Kuprin](/wiki/Aleksandr_Kuprin "Aleksandr Kuprin"), [Dmitry Merezhkovsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Merezhkovsky "Dmitry Merezhkovsky"), [Aleksey Remizov](/wiki/Aleksey_Remizov "Aleksey Remizov"), [Ivan Shmelyov](/wiki/Ivan_Shmelyov "Ivan Shmelyov"), [George Grebenstchikoff](/wiki/George_Grebenstchikoff "George Grebenstchikoff"), [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), and English-speaking [Ayn Rand](/wiki/Ayn_Rand "Ayn Rand"); and short-story [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")-winning writer and poet [Ivan Bunin](/wiki/Ivan_Bunin "Ivan Bunin"), continued to write in exile.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018536–542-82)

During his emigration, Bunin wrote his most significant works, such as his only autobiographical novel *[The Life of Arseniev](/wiki/The_Life_of_Arseniev "The Life of Arseniev")* (1927–1939) and short story cycle *[Dark Avenues](/wiki/Dark_Avenues "Dark Avenues")* (1937–1944). An example of long prose form is Grebenstchikoff's epic novel *The Churaevs* in six volumes (1922–1937) in which he described life of the [Siberians](/wiki/Siberians "Siberians").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasack1988-77) [M. Ageyev](/wiki/M._Ageyev "M. Ageyev") is known for his *[Novel with Cocaine](/wiki/Novel_with_Cocaine "Novel with Cocaine")* (1934). While the realists Bunin, Shmelyov and Grebenstchikoff wrote about the pre-revolutionary Russia, life of the émigrés was depicted in modernist Nabokov's *[Mary](/wiki/Mary_(Nabokov_novel) "Mary (Nabokov novel)")* (1926) *and [The Gift](/wiki/The_Gift_(Nabokov_novel) "The Gift (Nabokov novel)")* (1938), Gazdanov's *An Evening with Claire* (1929) and *The Specter of Alexander Wolf* (1948) and Georgy Ivanov's novel *Disintegration of the Atom* (1938).[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEgorovaFokinIvanova2014-83)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018536–542-82)

### Stalinist era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Stalinist era")]

In the 1930s, [Socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism") became the predominant official trend in the Soviet Union. Writers like those of the [Serapion Brothers](/wiki/Serapion_Brothers "Serapion Brothers") group (1921–), who insisted on the right of an author to write independently of political ideology, were forced by authorities to reject their views and accept socialist realist principles. Some 1930s writers, such as [Osip Mandelstam](/wiki/Osip_Mandelstam "Osip Mandelstam"), [Daniil Kharms](/wiki/Daniil_Kharms "Daniil Kharms"), leader of [Oberiu](/wiki/Oberiu "Oberiu"), [Leonid Dobychin](/wiki/Leonid_Dobychin "Leonid Dobychin"), [Mikhail Bulgakov](/wiki/Mikhail_Bulgakov "Mikhail Bulgakov"), author of *[The White Guard](/wiki/The_White_Guard "The White Guard")* (1923) and *[The Master and Margarita](/wiki/The_Master_and_Margarita "The Master and Margarita")* (1928–1940), and [Andrei Platonov](/wiki/Andrei_Platonov "Andrei Platonov"), author of novels *[Chevengur](/wiki/Chevengur "Chevengur")* (1928) and *[The Foundation Pit](/wiki/The_Foundation_Pit "The Foundation Pit")* (1930) were attacked by the official critics as "formalists," "naturalists" and ideological enemies and wrote with little or no hope of being published. Such remarkable writers as [Isaac Babel](/wiki/Isaac_Babel "Isaac Babel"), [Boris Pilnyak](/wiki/Boris_Pilnyak "Boris Pilnyak"), [Nikolai Klyuev](/wiki/Nikolai_Klyuev "Nikolai Klyuev"), [Sergey Klychkov](/wiki/Sergey_Klychkov "Sergey Klychkov"), Pyotr Oreshin and [Artyom Vesyoly](/wiki/Artyom_Vesyoly "Artyom Vesyoly"), who continued to publish their works but could not get used to the socrealist principles by the end of the 1930s, were executed on fabricated charges, and Osip Mandelstam, Daniil Kharms and [Alexander Vvedensky](/wiki/Alexander_Vvedensky_(poet) "Alexander Vvedensky (poet)") died in prison.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)

The return from emigration such famous authors as [Aleksey Tolstoy](/wiki/Aleksey_Nikolayevich_Tolstoy "Aleksey Nikolayevich Tolstoy"), [Maxim Gorky](/wiki/Maxim_Gorky "Maxim Gorky"), and [Ilya Ehrenburg](/wiki/Ilya_Ehrenburg "Ilya Ehrenburg") was a major propaganda victory for the Soviets. After his return to Russia Maxim Gorky was proclaimed by the Soviet authorities as "the founder of Socialist Realism". His novel [*Mother*](/wiki/Mother_(novel) "Mother (novel)") (1906), which Gorky himself considered one of his biggest failures, inspired proletarian writers to found the socrealist movement. Gorky defined socialist realism as the "realism of people who are rebuilding the world" and pointed out that it looks at the past "from the heights of the future's goals", although he defined it not as a strict style (which is studied in [Andrei Sinyavsky](/wiki/Andrei_Sinyavsky "Andrei Sinyavsky")'s essay *On Socialist Realism*), but as a label for the "union of writers of styles", who write for one purpose, to help in the development of the [new man](/wiki/New_Soviet_man "New Soviet man") in socialist society. Gorky became the initiator of creating the Writer's Union, a state organization, intended to unite the socrealist writers.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Despite the official reputation, Gorky's post-revolutionary works, such as the novel *[The Life of Klim Samgin](/wiki/The_Life_of_Klim_Samgin "The Life of Klim Samgin")* (1925–1936) can't be defined as socrealist, but [modernist](/wiki/Literary_modernism "Literary modernism").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreeborn1982[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidKGhGf-erYM8Cqsamgin_178]-86)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEgorovaFokinIvanova2014-83)

[Andrei Bely](/wiki/Andrei_Bely "Andrei Bely") (1880–1934), author of *[Petersburg](/wiki/Petersburg_(novel) "Petersburg (novel)")* (1913/1922), a well-known modernist writer, also was a member of Writer's Union and tried to become a "true" socrealist by writing a series of articles and making ideological revisions to his memoirs, and he also planned to begin a study of Socialist realism; however, he continued writing with his unique techniques.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Although he was actively published during his lifetime, his major works would not be reissued until the end of the 1970s. [Valentin Kataev](/wiki/Valentin_Kataev "Valentin Kataev"), who began publishing before the Revolution, is the author of the first Soviet "industrial novel" *[Time, Forward!](/wiki/Time,_Forward!_(novel) "Time, Forward! (novel)")* (1932) and the classic 1946 short story *Our Father*.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown1982101–2-88) [Mikhail Sholokhov](/wiki/Mikhail_Sholokhov "Mikhail Sholokhov") (1905–1984) was one of the most significant figures in the official Soviet literature. His main socrealist work is *Virgin Soil Upturned* (1935), a novel in which Sholokhov glorifies the collectivization. However, his unique for period best-known and the most significant literary achievement is *[Quiet Flows the Don](/wiki/And_Quiet_Flows_the_Don "And Quiet Flows the Don")* (1928–40), an epic novel which realistically depicts the life of [Don Cossacks](/wiki/Don_Cossacks "Don Cossacks") during the First World War, the Russian Revolution, and Russian Civil War.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

[Nikolai Ostrovsky](/wiki/Nikolai_Ostrovsky "Nikolai Ostrovsky")'s novel *[How the Steel Was Tempered](/wiki/How_the_Steel_Was_Tempered "How the Steel Was Tempered")* (1932–1934) has been among the most popular and standard works of literary socrealism,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) with tens of millions of copies printed in many languages around the world. In China, various versions of the book have sold more than 10 million copies.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In Russia more than 35 million copies of the book are in circulation.[[91]](#cite_note-compuart-91) The book is a fictionalized autobiography of Ostrovsky's life: he had a difficult working-class childhood, became a [Komsomol](/wiki/Komsomol "Komsomol") member in July 1919 and volunteered to join the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army"). The novel's protagonist, Pavel Korchagin, represented the "young hero" of Russian literature: he is dedicated to his political causes, which help him to overcome his tragedies.[[92]](#cite_note-92) [Alexander Fadeyev](/wiki/Alexander_Fadeyev_(writer) "Alexander Fadeyev (writer)") (1901–1956) was also a well-known Socialist realism writer, the chairman of the official Writer's Union during Stalinist era.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84) His novel *The Rout* (1927) deals with the partisan struggle in [Russia's Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East") during the Russian Revolution and Civil War of 1917–1922. Fadeyev described the theme of this novel as one of a revolution significantly transforming the [masses](/wiki/Commoner "Commoner").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1953-65)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)

In the 1930s, [Konstantin Paustovsky](/wiki/Konstantin_Paustovsky "Konstantin Paustovsky") (1892–1968), an influenced by [neo-Romantic](/wiki/Neo-romanticism "Neo-romanticism") works of [Alexander Grin](/wiki/Alexander_Grin "Alexander Grin") master of landscape prose, a singer of the [Meshchera Lowlands](/wiki/Meshchera_Lowlands "Meshchera Lowlands"), and already in the post-Stalin years a multiple nominee for the Nobel Prize in Literature, joined the ranks of leading Soviet writers.fantastic.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018599–605Neo-Romanticism-93) Novelist and playwright [Leonid Leonov](/wiki/Leonid_Leonov "Leonid Leonov"), despite the fact that he was considered by authorities to be one of the pillars of socialist realism,[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJonesMiller199813-94) during the Stalin years, created a forbidden novella about emigrants *Eugenua Ivanovna* (1938), a play about the [Chekist](/wiki/Chekism "Chekism") purges, *The Snowstorm* (1940), briefly permitted and then also forbidden, and a novel, *The Russian Forest* (1953), where ecological issues were perhaps touched upon for the first time in Soviet literature. Over the course of forty years (1940–1994), he wrote a huge philosophical and mystical novel, "The Pyramid", which was finished and published in the year of the author's death.

> Wait for me and I'll come back,  
> Escaping every fate!  
> "Just got lucky!" they will say,  
> Those that didn't wait.  
> They will never understand  
> How, amidst the strife,  
> By your waiting for me, dear,  
> You had saved my life!

—[Konstantin Simonov](/wiki/Konstantin_Simonov "Konstantin Simonov"), *[Wait for Me!](/wiki/Wait_for_Me_(poem) "Wait for Me (poem)")* (1941), translated by Mike Munford[[95]](#cite_note-95)

The cult figures of the literature of the Second World War were the [war poets](/wiki/War_poet "War poet") [Konstantin Simonov](/wiki/Konstantin_Simonov "Konstantin Simonov"), arguably most famous for his 1941 poem *[Wait for Me](/wiki/Wait_for_Me_(poem) "Wait for Me (poem)")*,[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeldernStites1995335-96) and [Aleksandr Tvardovsky](/wiki/Aleksandr_Tvardovsky "Aleksandr Tvardovsky"), author of the long poem "[Vasily Terkin](/wiki/Vasily_Terkin "Vasily Terkin")" (1941–45), chief editor of the literary magazine *[Novy Mir](/wiki/Novy_Mir "Novy Mir")*.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeldernStites1995371-97) Poet [Yulia Drunina](/wiki/Yulia_Drunina "Yulia Drunina") known for writing about women at war. [Boris Polevoy](/wiki/Boris_Polevoy "Boris Polevoy") is the author of the *Story About a True Man* (1946), based on the life of World War II fighter pilot [Aleksey Maresyev](/wiki/Aleksey_Maresyev "Aleksey Maresyev"), which was an immensely popular.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeldernStites1995416–421-98)

### Late Soviet era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Late Soviet era")]

> So what is beauty? And why does the human race  
> Keep up its worship, whether valid or misguided?  
> Is it a vessel holding empty space,  
> Or is it fire shimmering inside it?

[Nikolay Zabolotsky](/wiki/Nikolay_Zabolotsky "Nikolay Zabolotsky"), *A Plain Girl* (1955), translated by Alyona Mokraya[[99]](#cite_note-99)

After the end of World War II Nobel Prize-winning [Boris Pasternak](/wiki/Boris_Pasternak "Boris Pasternak") (1890–1960) wrote a novel [*Doctor Zhivago*](/wiki/Doctor_Zhivago_(novel) "Doctor Zhivago (novel)") (1945–1955). Publication of the novel in Italy caused a scandal, as the Soviet authorities forced Pasternak to renounce his 1958 Nobel Prize and denounced as an internal White emigre and a Fascist fifth columnist. Pasternak was expelled from the Writer's Union.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)

The majority of members of the Writers' Union ([Georgi Markov](/wiki/Georgi_Markov_(Soviet_writer) "Georgi Markov (Soviet writer)"), [Anatoly Rybakov](/wiki/Anatoly_Rybakov "Anatoly Rybakov"), [Aleksandr Chakovsky](/wiki/Aleksandr_Chakovsky "Aleksandr Chakovsky"), [Sergey Zalygin](/wiki/Sergey_Zalygin "Sergey Zalygin"), Anatoly Kalinin, [Daniil Granin](/wiki/Daniil_Granin "Daniil Granin"), [Yuri Nagibin](/wiki/Yuri_Nagibin "Yuri Nagibin"), [Vladimir Tendryakov](/wiki/Vladimir_Tendryakov "Vladimir Tendryakov"), Arkady Lvov (before his emigration), [Chinghiz Aitmatov](/wiki/Chinghiz_Aitmatov "Chinghiz Aitmatov"), [Anatoly Ivanov](/wiki/Anatoli_Ivanov_(writer) "Anatoli Ivanov (writer)"), Pyotr Proskurin, Boris Yekimov, among many others) continued to work in the mainstream of Socialist Realism, not without criticizing certain phenomena of Soviet reality, such as showiness, mismanagement, nepotism, and widespread poaching. However, even in officially recognized literature, not entirely canonical "mutations"—the natural [Lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant_prose "Lieutenant prose"), nostalgic [Village](/wiki/Village_Prose "Village Prose") and intellectual "Urban Prose" ([Yury Trifonov](/wiki/Yury_Trifonov "Yury Trifonov")), the literature of the [Sixtiers](/wiki/Sixtiers "Sixtiers") and "Quiet Poetry" movements appear. Since the 1960s, [Valentin Kataev](/wiki/Valentin_Kataev "Valentin Kataev") has been moving away from official realism, developing his own modernist style, "Mauvism" (from the French word *mauvais*, "bad").

> And however long the blizzard blows, whether it's three days or a week, every single day is counted as a day off, and the men are turned out to work Sunday after Sunday to make up for lost time.

[Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn"), *[One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich](/wiki/One_Day_in_the_Life_of_Ivan_Denisovich "One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich")* (1962), translated by [H. T. Willetts](/wiki/Harry_Willetts "Harry Willetts")[[100]](#cite_note-100)

The [Khrushchev Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw") (c. 1954 – c. 1964) brought some fresh wind to literature (the term was coined after [Ilya Ehrenburg](/wiki/Ilya_Ehrenburg "Ilya Ehrenburg")'s 1954 novel *[The Thaw](/wiki/The_Thaw_(Ehrenburg_novel) "The Thaw (Ehrenburg novel)")*). Published in 1956, [Vladimir Dudintsev](/wiki/Vladimir_Dudintsev "Vladimir Dudintsev")'s novel *[Not by Bread Alone](/wiki/Not_by_Bread_Alone "Not by Bread Alone")* and [Yury Dombrovsky](/wiki/Yury_Dombrovsky "Yury Dombrovsky")'s *The Keeper of Antiquities* in 1964 became two of the main literary events of the Thaw and a milestone in the process of [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization"), but was soon criticized and withdrawn from circulation.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasack1988-77) The last years of life were fruitful for [Nikolay Zabolotsky](/wiki/Nikolay_Zabolotsky "Nikolay Zabolotsky"), who was repressed during the Stalin years. The publication in 1962 of the philosophical novelist [Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn")'s debut story *[One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich](/wiki/One_Day_in_the_Life_of_Ivan_Denisovich "One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich")* about a political prisoner became a national and international sensation.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) Poetry of the [Sixtiers](/wiki/Sixtiers "Sixtiers") or Russian New Wave became a [mass-cultural](/wiki/Mass_culture "Mass culture") phenomenon: [Bella Akhmadulina](/wiki/Bella_Akhmadulina "Bella Akhmadulina"), [Boris Slutsky](/wiki/Boris_Slutsky "Boris Slutsky"), [Victor Sosnora](/wiki/Victor_Sosnora "Victor Sosnora"), [Robert Rozhdestvensky](/wiki/Robert_Rozhdestvensky "Robert Rozhdestvensky"), [Andrei Voznesensky](/wiki/Andrei_Voznesensky "Andrei Voznesensky"), and [Yevgeny Yevtushenko](/wiki/Yevgeny_Yevtushenko "Yevgeny Yevtushenko"), read their poems in stadiums and attracted huge crowds, as follows:

> I don’t know about the rest of you,  
> but I feel the cruelest  
> nostalgia — not for the past —  
> but nostalgia for the present.

— [Andrei Voznesensky](/wiki/Andrei_Voznesensky "Andrei Voznesensky"), *Nostalgia for the Present* (1976), translated by Vera Dunham and H. W. Tjalsma[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Such exponents of neo-[Acmeist poetry](/wiki/Acmeist_poetry "Acmeist poetry") as [Arseny Tarkovsky](/wiki/Arseny_Tarkovsky "Arseny Tarkovsky"), [Semyon Lipkin](/wiki/Semyon_Lipkin "Semyon Lipkin"), [David Samoylov](/wiki/David_Samoylov "David Samoylov"), [Alexander Kushner](/wiki/Alexander_Kushner "Alexander Kushner") and [Oleg Chukhontsev](/wiki/Oleg_Chukhontsev "Oleg Chukhontsev"),[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018578-102) the representatives "quiet poetry" Anatoly Zhigulin, [Stanislav Kunyaev](/wiki/Stanislav_Kunyaev "Stanislav Kunyaev"), [Nikolay Rubtsov](/wiki/Nikolay_Rubtsov "Nikolay Rubtsov") and Yury Kuznetsov, and also Gleb Gorbovsky, [bard](/wiki/Bard_(Soviet_Union) "Bard (Soviet Union)") [Novella Matveyeva](/wiki/Novella_Matveyeva "Novella Matveyeva"), [Yunna Morits](/wiki/Yunna_Morits "Yunna Morits"), and Gleb Semenov's lyrical poetry also stood apart from the socrealist mainstream.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKasack1988-77)

The [Village Prose](/wiki/Village_Prose "Village Prose") was a movement in Soviet literature beginning during the Khrushchev Thaw, which included works that cultivated nostalgia of rural life.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthé1992-103)[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin2000225–242-104) [Valentin Ovechkin](/wiki/Valentin_Ovechkin "Valentin Ovechkin")'s story *District Routine* (1952), expose managerial inefficiency, the self-interest of party functionaries,[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras1985326–327-105) was the starting point of the movement.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthé1992151-106)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin2000225-107) Its major members [Alexander Yashin](/wiki/Alexander_Yashin "Alexander Yashin"), [Fyodor Abramov](/wiki/Fyodor_Abramov "Fyodor Abramov"), [Boris Mozhayev](/wiki/Boris_Mozhayev "Boris Mozhayev"), [Viktor Astafyev](/wiki/Viktor_Astafyev "Viktor Astafyev"), [Vladimir Soloukhin](/wiki/Vladimir_Soloukhin "Vladimir Soloukhin"), [Vasily Shukshin](/wiki/Vasily_Shukshin "Vasily Shukshin"), [Vasily Belov](/wiki/Vasily_Belov "Vasily Belov"), and [Valentin Rasputin](/wiki/Valentin_Rasputin "Valentin Rasputin") clustered in the traditionalist and nationalist *[Nash Sovremennik](/wiki/Nash_Sovremennik "Nash Sovremennik")* literary magazine.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosgrove2004151-108)

Since 1985/86, the *[Perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")*—a period of great changes in the political and cultural life in the USSR—gave way to a wide diversity of banned previously and new writings.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin20002-109)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinGenisVladiv-Glover2016151-110)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018560-111) In 1986 there was established the legal non-Realistic literary club "Poetry", among its members were [Dmitry Prigov](/wiki/Dmitry_Prigov "Dmitry Prigov"), [Igor Irtenyev](/wiki/Igor_Irtenyev "Igor Irtenyev"), Aleksandr Yeryomenko, [Sergey Gandlevsky](/wiki/Sergey_Gandlevsky "Sergey Gandlevsky"), and Yuri Arabov. Many previously suppressed works were published[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) among first, in 1986–87, anti-Stalinist [Alexander Bek](/wiki/Alexander_Bek "Alexander Bek")'s novel *The New Appointment* (1965)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerras198543-112) and Anatoly Rybakov's *[Children of the Arbat](/wiki/Children_of_the_Arbat "Children of the Arbat")* trilogy. The events of the [theater of the absurd](/wiki/Theater_of_the_absurd "Theater of the absurd") were postmodern plays of [Nina Sadur](/wiki/Nina_Sadur "Nina Sadur"). Among the best writers of "alternative fiction," openly discussing previously taboo themes, were Mikhail Kurayev (b. 1939), [Valery Popov](/wiki/Valery_Popov_(writer) "Valery Popov (writer)"), [Tatyana Tolstaya](/wiki/Tatyana_Tolstaya "Tatyana Tolstaya"), and [Viktor Yerofeyev](/wiki/Viktor_Yerofeyev "Viktor Yerofeyev").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)

#### Soviet nonconformism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Soviet nonconformism")]

See also: [Soviet nonconformist art](/wiki/Soviet_nonconformist_art "Soviet nonconformist art")
