Some writers dared to oppose Soviet ideology, like short-story writer [Varlam Shalamov](/wiki/Varlam_Shalamov "Varlam Shalamov") (1907–1982) and Nobel Prize-winning novelist [Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn") (1918–2008), who wrote about life in the [gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") camps, or [Vasily Grossman](/wiki/Vasily_Grossman "Vasily Grossman") (1905–1964), with his description of World War II events countering the Soviet official historiography (his epic novel *[Life and Fate](/wiki/Life_and_Fate "Life and Fate")* (1959) was not published in the Soviet Union until the *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")*). Such writers, dubbed "[dissidents](/wiki/Dissidents "Dissidents")", could not publish their major works until the 1960s.[[113]](#cite_note-accursed-113)

Modernist and [Postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_literature "Postmodern literature") dissident literature[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018688–694-114) was related and partially coincided with the [Soviet nonconformist art](/wiki/Soviet_nonconformist_art "Soviet nonconformist art") movement. From 1953 to 1957, the *Mansard Group*—first unofficial poetry group—existed till its leader Leonid Chertkov (1933–2000) was imprisoned, among other members Galina Andreeva (1933–2016) and Stanislav Krasovitsky (b. 1935). Another poetry group of '50s in Leningrad was the *Philological School* that included Mikhail Eremin (1936–2022), Sergey Kulle (1936–1984), Leonid Vinogradov (1936–2004) and poet and artist [Vladimir Uflyand](/wiki/Vladimir_Uflyand "Vladimir Uflyand") (1937–2007). Some poets were both artists or participants and inspirers of art groups, such as Evgenii Kropivnitsky (1893–1979), [Igor Kholin](/wiki/Igor_Kholin "Igor Kholin"), [Genrikh Sapgir](/wiki/Genrikh_Sapgir "Genrikh Sapgir"), [Vilen Barskyi](/wiki/Vilen_Barskyi "Vilen Barskyi") (1930–2012), Roald Mandelstam (1932–1961), Vsevolod Nekrasov (1934–2009), Mikhail Eremin (1936–2022), [Igor Sinyavin](/wiki/Igor_Sinyavin "Igor Sinyavin") (1937–2000), [Alexei Khvostenko](/wiki/Alexei_Khvostenko "Alexei Khvostenko") (1940–2004), [Dmitry Prigov](/wiki/Dmitri_Prigov "Dmitri Prigov") (1940–2007), Kari Unksova (1941–1983), [Ry Nikonova](/wiki/Ry_Nikonova "Ry Nikonova") (1942–2014), [Oleg Grigoriev](/wiki/Oleg_Grigoriev "Oleg Grigoriev") (1943–1992), Valery Kholodenko (1945–1993), [Serge Segay](/wiki/Serge_Segay "Serge Segay") (1947–2014), and [Vladimir Sorokin](/wiki/Vladimir_Sorokin "Vladimir Sorokin") (b. 1955).[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenfeldDodge1995-115)

But the late 1950s thaw did not last long. In the 1970s, some of the most prominent authors were not only banned from publishing but were also prosecuted for their anti-Soviet sentiments, or for [parasitism](/wiki/Parasitism "Parasitism"), thus writers [Yuli Daniel](/wiki/Yuli_Daniel "Yuli Daniel") (1925–1988) and [Leonid Borodin](/wiki/Leonid_Borodin "Leonid Borodin") (1938–2011) was imprisoned. Solzhenitsyn and Nobel Prize–winning poet [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky") (1940–1996) were expelled from the country.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27) Others, such as writers and poets David Dar (1910–1980), [Viktor Nekrasov](/wiki/Viktor_Nekrasov "Viktor Nekrasov") (1911–1987), [Lev Kopelev](/wiki/Lev_Kopelev "Lev Kopelev") (1912–1997), [Aleksandr Galich](/wiki/Alexander_Galich_(writer) "Alexander Galich (writer)") (1918–1977), [Arkadiy Belinkov](/wiki/Arkadiy_Belinkov "Arkadiy Belinkov") (1921–2019), Elizaveta Mnatsakanova (1922–2006), [Alexander Zinoviev](/wiki/Alexander_Zinoviev "Alexander Zinoviev") (1922–2006), [Naum Korzhavin](/wiki/Naum_Korzhavin "Naum Korzhavin") (1925–2018), [Andrei Sinyavsky](/wiki/Andrei_Sinyavsky "Andrei Sinyavsky") (1925–1997), Arkady Lvov (1927–2020), [Yuz Aleshkovsky](/wiki/Yuz_Aleshkovsky "Yuz Aleshkovsky") (1929–2022), [Anatoly Kuznetsov](/wiki/Anatoly_Kuznetsov "Anatoly Kuznetsov") (1929–1979), Vilen Barskyi, [Vladimir Maksimov](/wiki/Vladimir_Maksimov_(writer) "Vladimir Maksimov (writer)") (1930–1995), [Yuri Mamleev](/wiki/Yuri_Mamleev "Yuri Mamleev") (1931–2015), [Georgi Vladimov](/wiki/Georgi_Vladimov "Georgi Vladimov") (1931–2003), [Vasily Aksyonov](/wiki/Vasily_Aksyonov "Vasily Aksyonov") (1932–2009), [Vladimir Voinovich](/wiki/Vladimir_Voinovich "Vladimir Voinovich") (1932–2018), Leonid Chertkov, [Anatoly Gladilin](/wiki/Anatoly_Gladilin "Anatoly Gladilin") (1935–2018), [Anri Volokhonsky](/wiki/Anri_Volokhonsky "Anri Volokhonsky") (1936–2017), Vadim Kreid (b. 1936), [Andrei Bitov](/wiki/Andrei_Bitov "Andrei Bitov") (1937–2018), Igor Sinyavin, Alexei Khvostenko, [Sergei Dovlatov](/wiki/Sergei_Dovlatov "Sergei Dovlatov") (1941–1990), [Eduard Limonov](/wiki/Eduard_Limonov "Eduard Limonov") (1943–2020), and [Sasha Sokolov](/wiki/Sasha_Sokolov "Sasha Sokolov") (b. 1943), had to emigrate to the West,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018536–542-82) while Oleg Grigoriev and [Venedikt Yerofeyev](/wiki/Venedict_Yerofeyev "Venedict Yerofeyev") (1938–1990) "emigrated" to alcoholism, and repressed still in Stalinist years poet Yury Aikhenvald (1928–1993) with some others to translations, and Kari Unksova and [Yury Dombrovsky](/wiki/Yury_Dombrovsky "Yury Dombrovsky") (1909–1978) were murdered, Dombrovsky shortly after publishing his novel *[The Faculty of Useless Knowledge](/wiki/The_Faculty_of_Useless_Knowledge "The Faculty of Useless Knowledge")* (1975). Their books were not published officially until the *perestroika* period of the 1980s, although fans continued to reprint them manually in a manner called "*[samizdat](/wiki/Samizdat "Samizdat")*" (self-publishing).[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018554–557-116)

In 1960s arose unofficial Soviet [second Russian avant-garde](/wiki/Second_Russian_avant-garde "Second Russian avant-garde") and [Russian postmodernism](/wiki/Russian_postmodernism "Russian postmodernism"). In 1965–72, at Leningrad existed the avantgardist [Absurdist](/wiki/Absurdist_fiction "Absurdist fiction") poetic and writing group "Khelenkuts", which included Vladimir Erl and Aleksandr Mironov, among others. Andrei Bitov was Postmodernism first proponent. In 1970, Venedikt Erofeyev's [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") postmodern prose poem *[Moscow-Petushki](/wiki/Moscow-Petushki "Moscow-Petushki")* was published via *samizdat*.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinGenisVladiv-Glover201695-117)[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018693–694-118) The Soviet emigrant Sasha Sokolov wrote surrealist *[A School for Fools](/wiki/A_School_for_Fools "A School for Fools")* in 1973 and the completely postmodern novel *Between Dog and Wolf* in 1980.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin2000218-119)
Other remarkable postmodern novels were Eduard Limonov's *[It's Me, Eddie](/wiki/It%27s_Me,_Eddie "It's Me, Eddie")*, Vladimir Voinovich's *[The Life and Extraordinary Adventures of Private Ivan Chonkin](/wiki/The_Life_and_Extraordinary_Adventures_of_Private_Ivan_Chonkin "The Life and Extraordinary Adventures of Private Ivan Chonkin")*, Vasily Aksyonov's *The Island of Crimea* and [Vladimir Sorokin](/wiki/Vladimir_Sorokin "Vladimir Sorokin")'s *The Norm*. Sergei Dovlatov, [Valery Popov](/wiki/Valery_Popov_(writer) "Valery Popov (writer)"), and [Yevgeni Popov](/wiki/Yevgeni_Anatolyevich_Popov "Yevgeni Anatolyevich Popov") predominantly wrote short stories. Since '70s there were such postmodern unofficial movements as [Moscow Conceptualists](/wiki/Moscow_Conceptualists "Moscow Conceptualists") with elements of [concrete poetry](/wiki/Concrete_poetry "Concrete poetry")[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenfeldDodge1995332"A_View_from_Moscow"-120)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018631–635-121) (Vsevolod Nekrasov, Dmitry Prigov, writer and literary scholar [Viktor Yerofeyev](/wiki/Viktor_Yerofeyev "Viktor Yerofeyev"), [Lev Rubinstein](/wiki/Lev_Rubinstein "Lev Rubinstein"), Timur Kibirov, early Vladimir Sorokin) and [Metarealism](/wiki/Metarealism "Metarealism"), namely metaphysical realism, used complex metaphors which they called meta-metaphors (Konstantin Kedrov, [Viktor Krivulin](/wiki/Viktor_Krivulin "Viktor Krivulin"), Elena Katsyuba, Ivan Zhdanov, [Elena Shvarts](/wiki/Elena_Shvarts "Elena Shvarts"),[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018593–599-122) Vladimir Aristov, Aleksandr Yeryomenko, scholar Svetlana Kekova, [Yuri Arabov](/wiki/Yuri_Arabov "Yuri Arabov"), [Alexei Parshchikov](/wiki/Alexei_Parshchikov "Alexei Parshchikov"), Sergei Nadeem and Nikolai Kononov).[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonAshby199210,_53,_184-123)[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpsteinGenisVladiv-Glover2016169–176[httpwwwemoryeduINTELNETepmconceptmetarealhtml_Theses_on_Metarealism_and_Conceptualism]-124)[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018639–641-125) [Arkadii Dragomoshchenko](/wiki/Arkadii_Dragomoshchenko "Arkadii Dragomoshchenko") is considered the foremost representative of the [Language Poets](/wiki/Language_poets "Language poets") in Russian literature.[[126]](#cite_note-126) In [Yeysk](/wiki/Yeysk "Yeysk"), there was the "Transfurist" group of mixing verbal, [sound](/wiki/Sound_poetry "Sound poetry") and [visual poetry](/wiki/Visual_poetry "Visual poetry") (Ry Nikonova and Serge Segay, among others). As mentioned Leonid Vinogradov, as well as members of *List of characters* group Mikhail Faynerman and Ivan Akhmetyev were exponents of [Minimalist](/wiki/Minimalism#Literature "Minimalism") verse. The banned from publishing [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_language "Chuvash language") and Russian poet [Gennadiy Aygi](/wiki/Gennadiy_Aygi "Gennadiy Aygi") had been creating experimental surrealist verses[[113]](#cite_note-accursed-113) as follows:

> And we utter a few words — simply because  
> we’re scared of silence  
> and deem any movement dangerous

— [Gennadiy Aygi](/wiki/Gennadiy_Aygi "Gennadiy Aygi"), *Our Way*, translated by [Anatoly Kudryavitsky](/wiki/Anatoly_Kudryavitsky "Anatoly Kudryavitsky")[[113]](#cite_note-accursed-113)

Among other underground poets and writers were the exponent of [stream of consciousness](/wiki/Stream_of_consciousness "Stream of consciousness") prose [Pavel Ulitin](/wiki/Pavel_Ulitin "Pavel Ulitin"), [Dmitry Avaliani](/wiki/Dmitry_Avaliani "Dmitry Avaliani"), [Yevgeny Kharitonov](/wiki/Yevgeny_Kharitonov_(poet) "Yevgeny Kharitonov (poet)"), economist and poet Yevgeny Saburov, Elena Ignatova, Mikhail Aizenberg and [Yevgeny Bunimovich](/wiki/Yevgeny_Bunimovich "Yevgeny Bunimovich"), as well partially banned [Vladimir Dudintsev](/wiki/Vladimir_Dudintsev "Vladimir Dudintsev"), [Fazil Iskander](/wiki/Fazil_Iskander "Fazil Iskander") and [Olga Sedakova](/wiki/Olga_Sedakova_(poet) "Olga Sedakova (poet)").

### Popular Soviet genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Popular Soviet genres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/%D0%9A%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D0%A7%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C%D0%BC%D0%B8.jpg/250px-%D0%9A%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D0%A7%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C%D0%BC%D0%B8.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9A%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D0%A7%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%81_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C%D0%BC%D0%B8.jpg)

[Korney Chukovsky](/wiki/Korney_Chukovsky "Korney Chukovsky") and children, 1959

Children's literature in the Soviet Union counted as a major genre because of its educational role. A large share of early-Soviet children's books were poems: [Korney Chukovsky](/wiki/Korney_Chukovsky "Korney Chukovsky") (1882–1969), [Samuil Marshak](/wiki/Samuil_Marshak "Samuil Marshak") (1887–1964) and [Agnia Barto](/wiki/Agniya_Barto "Agniya Barto") (1906–1981) were among the most read poets. "Adult" poets, such as Mayakovsky and [Sergey Mikhalkov](/wiki/Sergey_Mikhalkov "Sergey Mikhalkov") (1913–2009), contributed to the genre as well. Some of the early Soviet children's prose consisted of loose adaptations of foreign [fairy-tales](/wiki/Fairy-tale "Fairy-tale") unknown in contemporary Russia. [Alexey N. Tolstoy](/wiki/Alexey_Nikolaevich_Tolstoy "Alexey Nikolaevich Tolstoy") (1882–1945) wrote *[Buratino](/wiki/Buratino "Buratino")*, a light-hearted and shortened adaptation of [Carlo Collodi](/wiki/Carlo_Collodi "Carlo Collodi")'s *[Pinocchio](/wiki/Pinocchio "Pinocchio")*. [Alexander Volkov](/wiki/Alexander_Melentyevich_Volkov "Alexander Melentyevich Volkov") (1891–1977) introduced [fantasy fiction](/wiki/Fantasy_fiction "Fantasy fiction") to Soviet children with his loose translation of [L. Frank Baum](/wiki/L._Frank_Baum "L. Frank Baum")'s *[The Wonderful Wizard of Oz](/wiki/The_Wonderful_Wizard_of_Oz "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz")*, published as *[The Wizard of the Emerald City](/wiki/The_Wizard_of_the_Emerald_City "The Wizard of the Emerald City")* in 1939, and then wrote a series of five sequels, unrelated to Baum. Other notable authors include [Nikolay Nosov](/wiki/Nikolay_Nosov "Nikolay Nosov") (1908–1976), [Lazar Lagin](/wiki/Lazar_Lagin "Lazar Lagin") (1903–1979), [Vitaly Bianki](/wiki/Vitaly_Bianki "Vitaly Bianki") (1894–1959) and [Vladimir Suteev](/wiki/Vladimir_Suteev "Vladimir Suteev") (1903–1993).[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)

While fairy tales were relatively free from ideological oppression, the realistic children's prose of the Stalinist era was highly ideological and pursued the goal to raise children as [patriots](/wiki/Soviet_patriotism "Soviet patriotism") and communists. A notable writer in this vein was [Arkady Gaydar](/wiki/Arkady_Gaydar "Arkady Gaydar") (1904–1941), himself a Red Army commander (colonel) in [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"): his stories and plays about [Timur](/wiki/Timur_Gaidar "Timur Gaidar") describe a team of [young pioneer](/wiki/Young_Pioneer_organization_of_the_Soviet_Union "Young Pioneer organization of the Soviet Union") volunteers who help the elderly and resist [hooligans](/wiki/Hooliganism "Hooliganism").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84) There was a genre of hero-pioneer story that bore some similarities with Christian genre of [hagiography](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography"). In the times of [Khrushchov](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") ([First Secretary of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") from 1953 to 1964) and of [Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev") (in power 1966–1982), however, the pressure lightened. Mid- and late-Soviet children's books by [Eduard Uspensky](/wiki/Eduard_Uspensky "Eduard Uspensky"), Yuri Entin, Viktor Dragunsky bear no signs of propaganda. In the 1970s many of these books, as well as stories by foreign children's writers, were adapted into animation. The famous and widely popular [satirists](/wiki/Satire "Satire") were [Mikhail Zoshchenko](/wiki/Mikhail_Zoshchenko "Mikhail Zoshchenko"), [Valentin Kataev](/wiki/Valentin_Kataev "Valentin Kataev") and the writing tandem [Ilf and Petrov](/wiki/Ilf_and_Petrov "Ilf and Petrov"), described problems of post-Revolutionary Soviet society.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)

Soviet [Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction"), inspired by scientistic revolution, industrialisation, and the country's [space pioneering](/wiki/Soviet_space_program "Soviet space program"), was flourishing, albeit in the limits allowed by censors. Early science fiction authors, such as [Alexander Belyaev](/wiki/Alexander_Belyaev "Alexander Belyaev"), [Grigory Adamov](/wiki/Grigory_Adamov "Grigory Adamov"), [Vladimir Obruchev](/wiki/Vladimir_Obruchev "Vladimir Obruchev"), [Aleksey Nikolayevich Tolstoy](/wiki/Aleksey_Nikolayevich_Tolstoy "Aleksey Nikolayevich Tolstoy"), stuck to [hard science fiction](/wiki/Hard_science_fiction "Hard science fiction") and regarded [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") and [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne") as examples to follow. Two notable exceptions to this trend were early [Soviet dissidents](/wiki/Soviet_dissidents "Soviet dissidents") [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin"), author of [dystopian novel](/wiki/Utopian_and_dystopian_fiction "Utopian and dystopian fiction") *[We](/wiki/We_(novel) "We (novel)")*, and [Mikhail Bulgakov](/wiki/Mikhail_Bulgakov "Mikhail Bulgakov"), who used science fiction in *[Heart of a Dog](/wiki/Heart_of_a_Dog "Heart of a Dog")*, *[The Fatal Eggs](/wiki/The_Fatal_Eggs "The Fatal Eggs")* and *[Ivan Vasilyevich](/wiki/Ivan_Vasilyevich:_Back_to_the_Future "Ivan Vasilyevich: Back to the Future")* to satirize Communist ideology vs. what it is actual practice. Like the dissident writers of the future, Zamyatin and Bulgakov had serious problems with publishing their books due to [censorship in the Soviet Union](/wiki/Censorship_in_the_Soviet_Union "Censorship in the Soviet Union").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)

Since the [Khrushchev thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_thaw "Khrushchev thaw") in the 1950s, Soviet science fiction began to form its own style. Philosophy, [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") and [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia") ideas became its core, and [Social science fiction](/wiki/Social_science_fiction "Social science fiction") was the most popular subgenre.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlonim1977-84)[[127]](#cite_note-127) Although the view of Earth's future as that of utopian communist society was the only view that was welcome, the liberties of genre still offered a loophole for free expression. Books of brothers [Arkady and Boris Strugatsky](/wiki/Arkady_and_Boris_Strugatsky "Arkady and Boris Strugatsky"), and [Kir Bulychev](/wiki/Kir_Bulychev "Kir Bulychev"), among others, are reminiscent of social problems and often include satire of contemporary Soviet society. [Ivan Yefremov](/wiki/Ivan_Yefremov "Ivan Yefremov"), on the contrary, arose to fame with his [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") views on future as well as on [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") in his [historical novels](/wiki/Historical_novels "Historical novels"). The Strugatskies are also credited for the Soviet's first [science fantasy](/wiki/Science_fantasy "Science fantasy"), the [Monday Begins on Saturday](/wiki/Monday_Begins_on_Saturday "Monday Begins on Saturday") trilogy. Other notable science fiction writers included [Vladimir Savchenko](/wiki/Volodymyr_Ivanovych_Savchenko "Volodymyr Ivanovych Savchenko"), Georgy Gurevich, [Alexander Kazantsev](/wiki/Alexander_Kazantsev "Alexander Kazantsev"), Georgy Martynov, [Yeremey Parnov](/wiki/Yeremey_Parnov "Yeremey Parnov").

[Space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera") was less developed, since both state censors and serious writers watched it unfavorably. Nevertheless, there were moderately successful attempts to adapt space westerns to Soviet soil. The first was Alexander Kolpakov with "Griada", after came [Sergey Snegov](/wiki/Sergey_Snegov "Sergey Snegov") with "Men Like Gods", among others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A specific branch of both science fiction and children's books appeared in mid-Soviet era: the children's science fiction. It was meant to educate children while entertaining them. The star of the genre was Bulychov, who, along with his adult books, created children's space adventure series about [Alisa Selezneva](/wiki/Alisa_Selezneva "Alisa Selezneva"), a teenage girl from the future. Others include [Nikolay Nosov](/wiki/Nikolay_Nosov "Nikolay Nosov") with his books about dwarf [Neznayka](/wiki/Neznayka "Neznayka"), Evgeny Veltistov, who wrote about [robot boy Electronic](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_the_Elektronic "The Adventures of the Elektronic"), Vitaly Melentyev, [Vladislav Krapivin](/wiki/Vladislav_Krapivin "Vladislav Krapivin"), [Vitaly Gubarev](/wiki/Vitaly_Gubarev "Vitaly Gubarev"). [Mystery](/wiki/Mystery_fiction "Mystery fiction") was another popular genre. [Detectives](/wiki/Detective_fiction "Detective fiction") by [Vayner Brothers](/wiki/Vayner_Brothers "Vayner Brothers") and [spy novels](/wiki/Spy_fiction "Spy fiction") by [Yulian Semyonov](/wiki/Yulian_Semyonov "Yulian Semyonov") were best-selling,[[128]](#cite_note-Khagi-128) and many of them were adapted into film or TV in the 1970s and 1980s. [Village Prose](/wiki/Village_Prose "Village Prose") is a genre that conveys nostalgic descriptions of rural life. [Valentin Rasputin](/wiki/Valentin_Rasputin "Valentin Rasputin")'s 1976 novel,
*Proshchaniye s Matyoroy* (*Farewell to Matyora*) depicted a village faced with destruction to make room for a hydroelectric plant.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParthé1992-103)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin2000225-107)

[Historical fiction](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") in the early Soviet era included a large share of [memoirs](/wiki/Memoirs "Memoirs"), fictionalized or not. [Valentin Katayev](/wiki/Valentin_Katayev "Valentin Katayev") and [Lev Kassil](/wiki/Lev_Kassil "Lev Kassil") wrote semi-autobiographic books about children's life in Tsarist Russia. [Vladimir Gilyarovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Gilyarovsky "Vladimir Gilyarovsky") wrote *Moscow and Muscovites*, about life in pre-revolutionary Moscow. There were also attempts to write an epic novel about the Revolution, similar to Leo Tolstoy's *War and Peace*, based on the writers' own experience. Aleksey Tolstoy's *[The Road to Calvary](/wiki/The_Road_to_Calvary "The Road to Calvary")* (1920–1941) and [Mikhail Sholokhov](/wiki/Mikhail_Sholokhov "Mikhail Sholokhov")'s *[And Quiet Flows the Don](/wiki/And_Quiet_Flows_the_Don "And Quiet Flows the Don")* (1928–1940) depict Russia from the start of the Revolution to the end of the Civil War. *The Road to Calvary* demonstrates the victory of socialist ideas, while *And Quiet Flows the Don* gives a realist and a brutal image. [Maxim Gorky](/wiki/Maxim_Gorky "Maxim Gorky")'s and [Andrei Bely](/wiki/Andrei_Bely "Andrei Bely")'s experimental novels *[The Life of Klim Samgin](/wiki/The_Life_of_Klim_Samgin "The Life of Klim Samgin")* (1925–1936)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreeborn1982[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidKGhGf-erYM8Cqsamgin_178]-86) and *Moscow* (1926–1931) trace the relationship of Russian *[intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia")* with the revolutionary movement. Mikhail Bulgakov conceived to write a trilogy about the Civil War, but wrote only the first part, *[The White Guard](/wiki/The_White_Guard "The White Guard")* (1923). [Yury Tynyanov](/wiki/Yury_Tynyanov "Yury Tynyanov") focused on fictional biographies of the Golden Age writers: *The Death of Vazir-Mukhtar* (1928) and *Pushkin* (1935–1943). The late Soviet historical fiction was dominated by World War II novels and short stories by authors such as the representatives of [Lieutenant prose](/wiki/Lieutenant_prose "Lieutenant prose") (such as [Vasil Bykov](/wiki/Vasil_Bykov "Vasil Bykov")), [Vasily Grossman](/wiki/Vasily_Grossman "Vasily Grossman"), [Konstantin Simonov](/wiki/Konstantin_Simonov "Konstantin Simonov"), [Boris Vasilyev](/wiki/Boris_Vasilyev_(writer) "Boris Vasilyev (writer)"), [Viktor Astafyev](/wiki/Viktor_Astafyev "Viktor Astafyev"), among others, based on the authors' own war experience. [Vasily Yan](/wiki/Vasily_Yan "Vasily Yan") and [Konstantin Badygin](/wiki/Konstantin_Badygin "Konstantin Badygin") are best known for their novels on Medieval Rus, and [Yury Tynyanov](/wiki/Yury_Tynyanov "Yury Tynyanov") for writing on Russian Empire. [Valentin Pikul](/wiki/Valentin_Pikul "Valentin Pikul") wrote about many different epochs and countries in an [Alexander Dumas](/wiki/Alexander_Dumas "Alexander Dumas")-inspired style. In the 1970s there appeared a relatively independent [Village Prose](/wiki/Village_Prose "Village Prose"), whose most prominent representatives were [Viktor Astafyev](/wiki/Viktor_Astafyev "Viktor Astafyev") and [Valentin Rasputin](/wiki/Valentin_Rasputin "Valentin Rasputin").

Any sort of fiction that dealt with the occult, either [horror](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction"), adult-oriented fantasy or [magic realism](/wiki/Magic_realism "Magic realism"), was unwelcome in Soviet Russia.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Until the 1980s very few books in these genres were written, and even fewer were published, although earlier books, such as by Gogol, were not banned. Of the rare exceptions, Bulgakov in *[Master and Margarita](/wiki/Master_and_Margarita "Master and Margarita")* (not published in author's lifetime) and Strugatskies in *[Monday Begins on Saturday](/wiki/Monday_Begins_on_Saturday "Monday Begins on Saturday")* introduced magic and mystical creatures into contemporary Soviet reality to satirize it. Another exception was early Soviet writer [Alexander Grin](/wiki/Alexander_Grin "Alexander Grin"), who wrote [neo-Romantic](/wiki/Neo-romanticism "Neo-romanticism") tales, both realistic and fantastic.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018599–605Neo-Romanticism-93)

## Bronze Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Bronze Age")]

### Post-Soviet 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Post-Soviet 1990s")]

The end of the 20th century proved a difficult period for Russian literature, with relatively few distinct voices. Although the limited censorship of the period of *[glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost")* was lifted, *de facto* since [1989 in the Soviet Union](/wiki/1989_in_the_Soviet_Union "1989 in the Soviet Union"), *de jure* in 1990, and writers could now freely express their thoughts, the political and economic chaos of the 1990s affected the book market and literature heavily. The book printing industry descended into crisis, the number of printed book copies dropped several times in comparison to Soviet era, and it took about a decade to revive. Some major thick [literary magazines](/wiki/Literary_magazine "Literary magazine") went bankrupt.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDobrenkoLipovetsky2015-130) And "writers' traditional special place in society no longer is recognised by most Russians..."[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurtisLeighton1998-27)

> My words are awkward, like farts at a funeral, but sincere, like screams during interrogations…

Vladimir Sorokin, *[Blue Lard](/wiki/Blue_Lard "Blue Lard")* (1999), translated by the Wikipedia editors

Among the most discussed figures of this period were authors [Victor Pelevin](/wiki/Victor_Pelevin "Victor Pelevin") (b. 1962), disputably related to [postmodernism](/wiki/Russian_postmodernism "Russian postmodernism")[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhagi20218-131) and the [New Sincerity](/wiki/New_sincerity "New sincerity") movement,[[132]](#cite_note-132) who is author of the [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen")-inspired *[Chapayev and the Void](/wiki/Chapayev_and_Void "Chapayev and Void")*, "the first novel which takes place in an absolute vacuum," postmodernist[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhagi20217-133) novelist and playwright [Vladimir Sorokin](/wiki/Vladimir_Sorokin "Vladimir Sorokin") (b. 1955, the novels *Their Four Hearts* and *[Blue Lard](/wiki/Blue_Lard "Blue Lard")*), who started an underground writing career still in the early 1980s,[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMillin2000299–310-134) and the [conceptualist](/wiki/Moscow_Conceptualists "Moscow Conceptualists")[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhagi20217-133) poet [Dmitry Prigov](/wiki/Dmitri_Prigov "Dmitri Prigov") (1940–2007). Among other significant Postmodern works are [Lyudmila Petrushevskaya](/wiki/Lyudmila_Petrushevskaya "Lyudmila Petrushevskaya")'s novella *[The Time: Night](/wiki/The_Time:_Night "The Time: Night")*, Anatoly Korolyov's novel *Eron*, [Yevgeni Popov](/wiki/Yevgeni_Anatolyevich_Popov "Yevgeni Anatolyevich Popov")'s novel *The Real Story of the "Green musicians"*, [Tatyana Tolstaya](/wiki/Tatyana_Tolstaya "Tatyana Tolstaya")'s novel *The Stynx*, [Vladimir Sharov](/wiki/Vladimir_Sharov "Vladimir Sharov")'s [historiosophical](/wiki/Philosophy_of_history "Philosophy of history") prose as well the Israeli literary scholar and later novelist [Alexander Goldstein](/wiki/Alexander_Goldstein_(writer) "Alexander Goldstein (writer)")'s variable essays.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDobrenkoLipovetsky2015-130)

The tradition of the classic Russian realistic novel with [modernist](/wiki/Literary_modernism "Literary modernism"), [magic realism](/wiki/Magic_realism "Magic realism") and new "postrealism" elements continues with such authors as: (1) the living Soviet classics [Leonid Leonov](/wiki/Leonid_Leonov "Leonid Leonov"), [Viktor Astafyev](/wiki/Viktor_Astafyev "Viktor Astafyev"), Yury Davydov (novel *The Bestseller*), [Valentin Rasputin](/wiki/Valentin_Rasputin "Valentin Rasputin"), [Viktoriya Tokareva](/wiki/Viktoriya_Tokareva "Viktoriya Tokareva") and [Vladimir Makanin](/wiki/Vladimir_Makanin "Vladimir Makanin"); (2) the Soviet nonconformists [Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn](/wiki/Aleksandr_Solzhenitsyn "Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn"), [Georgi Vladimov](/wiki/Georgi_Vladimov "Georgi Vladimov") (a novel *The General and His Army*) and [Vasily Aksyonov](/wiki/Vasily_Aksyonov "Vasily Aksyonov") (the trilogy *[Generations of Winter](/wiki/Generations_of_Winter "Generations of Winter")*); (3) "new wave" of playwright and theatre director [Nikolay Kolyada](/wiki/Nikolay_Kolyada "Nikolay Kolyada") (b. 1957), Aleksey Varlamov (b. 1963), Pavel Krusanov (b. 1961) and [Mikhail Shishkin](/wiki/Mikhail_Shishkin_(writer) "Mikhail Shishkin (writer)") (b. 1961). Short stories of [Sergei Dovlatov](/wiki/Sergei_Dovlatov "Sergei Dovlatov") who emigrated to the US in 1979 and died in 1990 became very popular in Russia posthumously. A relatively new trend in Russian literature is that female short story writers mentioned Viktoriya Tokareva and Lyudmila Petrushevskaya or Tatyana Tolstaya, and novelists [Lyudmila Ulitskaya](/wiki/Lyudmila_Ulitskaya "Lyudmila Ulitskaya"), [Nina Sadur](/wiki/Nina_Sadur "Nina Sadur"), Irina Polyanskaya (1952–2004), [Dina Rubina](/wiki/Dina_Rubina "Dina Rubina") or Valeriya Narbikova (b. 1958) have come into prominence.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELedkovskyRosenthalZirin1994-135)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoscilo1996-136)

Detective stories and thrillers have proven a very successful genre of new Russian literature: in the 1990s serial detective novels by [Alexandra Marinina](/wiki/Alexandra_Marinina "Alexandra Marinina"), [Polina Dashkova](/wiki/Polina_Dashkova "Polina Dashkova") and [Darya Dontsova](/wiki/Darya_Dontsova "Darya Dontsova") were published in millions of copies. In the next decade [Boris Akunin](/wiki/Boris_Akunin "Boris Akunin") who wrote more sophisticated popular fiction, e.g., a series of novels about the 19th century sleuth [Erast Fandorin](/wiki/Erast_Fandorin "Erast Fandorin"), was eagerly read across the country. Science fiction was always well selling, albeit second to [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy"), that was relatively new to Russian readers. These genres boomed in the late 1990s, with authors like [Sergey Lukyanenko](/wiki/Sergey_Lukyanenko "Sergey Lukyanenko"), [Nick Perumov](/wiki/Nick_Perumov "Nick Perumov"), [Maria Semenova](/wiki/Maria_Semenova "Maria Semenova"), [Vera Kamsha](/wiki/Vera_Kamsha "Vera Kamsha"), [Alexey Pehov](/w/index.php?title=Alexey_Pehov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alexey Pehov (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%85%D0%BE%D0%B2,_%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B5%D0%B9_%D0%AE%D1%80%D1%8C%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87 "ru:Пехов, Алексей Юрьевич")], [Anton Vilgotsky](/wiki/Tony_Vilgotsky "Tony Vilgotsky") and [Vadim Panov](/wiki/Vadim_Panov "Vadim Panov"). A good share of modern Russian science fiction and fantasy is written in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), especially in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv"),[[137]](#cite_note-137) home to [H. L. Oldie](/wiki/H._L._Oldie "H. L. Oldie"), [Alexander Zorich](/wiki/Alexander_Zorich "Alexander Zorich"), [Yuri Nikitin](/wiki/Yuri_Nikitin_(author) "Yuri Nikitin (author)") and [Andrey Valentinov](/wiki/Andrey_Valentinov "Andrey Valentinov"). Many others hail from Kyiv, including [Marina and Sergey Dyachenko](/wiki/Marina_and_Sergey_Dyachenko "Marina and Sergey Dyachenko") and [Vladimir Arenev](/wiki/Vladimir_Arenev "Vladimir Arenev"). Significant contribution to Russian horror literature has been done by Ukrainians [Andrey Dashkov](/wiki/Andrey_Dashkov "Andrey Dashkov") and Alexander Vargo. Russian poetry of that period produced a number of avant-garde greats. The [Moscow Conceptualists](/wiki/Moscow_Conceptualists "Moscow Conceptualists") and followers of [Concrete poetry](/wiki/Concrete_poetry "Concrete poetry"), such as mentioned Dmitry Prigov, [Lev Rubinstein](/wiki/Lev_Rubinstein "Lev Rubinstein"), [Anna Alchuk](/wiki/Anna_Alchuk "Anna Alchuk") and Timur Kibirov (also novelist and literary scholar [Viktor Yerofeyev](/wiki/Viktor_Yerofeyev "Viktor Yerofeyev")), and the members of the Lianosovo group of [nonconformist](/wiki/Soviet_nonconformist_art "Soviet nonconformist art") poets, notably [Genrikh Sapgir](/wiki/Genrikh_Sapgir "Genrikh Sapgir"), [Igor Kholin](/wiki/Igor_Kholin "Igor Kholin") and Vsevolod Nekrasov, who previously chose to refrain from publication in Soviet periodicals, became very influential, especially in Moscow,[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018631–635-121) and the same goes for another masterful experimental neo-[surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_language "Chuvash language") and Russian poet, [Gennadiy Aygi](/wiki/Gennadiy_Aygi "Gennadiy Aygi").[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138)

Also popular were poets following some other poetic trends, e.g., members of "neo-Baroque" poetry school (not to be confused with [neo-Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_Revival_architecture "Baroque Revival architecture")) Ivan Zhdanov, [Elena Shvarts](/wiki/Elena_Shvarts "Elena Shvarts"), Aleksandr Yeryomenko and [Alexei Parshchikov](/wiki/Alexei_Parshchikov "Alexei Parshchikov"), Konstantin Kedrov and Elena Katsuba from *DOOS*, scholar Svetlana Kekova, Sergei Nadeem and Nikolai Kononov from [Saratov](/wiki/Saratov "Saratov") club *Cocoon*, Vladimir Aristov, [Yuri Arabov](/wiki/Yuri_Arabov "Yuri Arabov") and other representatives of the 1970–80s [Metarealism](/wiki/Metarealism "Metarealism"), who all used complex metaphors which they called meta-metaphors;[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138)[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahnLipovetskyReyfmanSandler2018639–641-125) in St. Petersburg, members of *New Leningrad Poetry School* that included not only the famous [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky") but also [Viktor Krivulin](/wiki/Viktor_Krivulin "Viktor Krivulin"), Sergey Stratanovsky and Elena Shvarts, and such members of *Philological School* as Mikhail Eremin, Leonid Vinogradov, [Vladimir Uflyand](/wiki/Vladimir_Uflyand "Vladimir Uflyand") and the Russian-American scholar [Lev Loseff](/wiki/Lev_Loseff "Lev Loseff"), were prominent first in the Soviet-times underground—and later in mainstream poetry;[[113]](#cite_note-accursed-113)[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138) [minimalist](/wiki/Minimalism#Literature "Minimalism") verse was represented since 1970s by members of *List of characters* group Mikhail Faynerman, Ivan Akhmetyev and later by Alexander Makarov-Krotkov; in 1992 emerged, the Meloimaginist group related to previous [Imaginism](/wiki/Imaginism "Imaginism") and included such poets and novelists as Russian-Irish bilingual [Anatoly Kudryavitsky](/wiki/Anatoly_Kudryavitsky "Anatoly Kudryavitsky") and Ludmila Vaturina; among other names, poets with nonconformist background Russian-Austrian musicolog Elizaveta Mnatsakanova, Galina Andreeva, Leonid Chertkov, Stanislav Krasovitsky, [Dmitry Avaliani](/wiki/Dmitry_Avaliani "Dmitry Avaliani"), [Ry Nikonova](/wiki/Ry_Nikonova "Ry Nikonova"), economist Yevgeny Saburov, Russian-Israeli author Elena Ignatova, Mikhail Aizenberg, Yevgeny Bunimovich and Dimitry Grigoriev, also poet and writer Nikolaĭ Baĭtov, the Russian-German scholar Sergey Biryukov with futurist and surrealist background,[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138) Irina Iermakova, Vitaly Kalpidi, the unable to publish during Soviet years scholar [Olga Sedakova](/wiki/Olga_Sedakova_(poet) "Olga Sedakova (poet)"), and [Borys Khersonskyi](/wiki/Borys_Khersonskyi "Borys Khersonskyi"). Notable poets of younger generation are [Elena Fanailova](/wiki/Elena_Fanailova "Elena Fanailova") (b. 1962), German Lukomnikov (b. 1962), [Vera Pavlova](/wiki/Vera_Pavlova "Vera Pavlova") (b. 1963), Grigory Dashevsky (1964–2013), [Sergei Kruglov](/wiki/Sergei_Kruglov_(poet) "Sergei Kruglov (poet)") (b. 1966), [Dmitry Kuzmin](/wiki/Dmitry_Kuzmin "Dmitry Kuzmin") (b. 1968), Arseniy Rovinsky (b. 1968), Asya Shneiderman (b. 1968), [Maxim Amelin](/wiki/Maxim_Amelin "Maxim Amelin") (b. 1970), Mikhail Gronas (b. 1970), [Fyodor Svarovsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Svarovsky "Fyodor Svarovsky") (b. 1971), Stanislav Lvovsky (b. 1972), [Maria Stepanova](/wiki/Maria_Stepanova_(poet) "Maria Stepanova (poet)") (b. 1972), Alina Vitukhnovskaya (b. 1973),[[138]](#cite_note-20poets-138) Inga Kuznetsova (b. 1974), [Boris Ryzhy](/wiki/Boris_Ryzhy "Boris Ryzhy") (1974–2001), Shish Bryansky (b. 1975), [Linor Goralik](/wiki/Linor_Goralik "Linor Goralik") (b. 1975), [Kirill Medvedev](/wiki/Kirill_Medvedev "Kirill Medvedev") (b. 1975), and [Polina Barskova](/wiki/Polina_Barskova "Polina Barskova") (b. 1976).[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandler2024-139)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russian_literature&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 21st century")]

> —Why has our planet been selected?  
> —It has not been selected. It was created as a prison from the start.

Victor Pelevin, *[Empire V](/wiki/Empire_V "Empire V")* (2006)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhagi20213-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Vladimir_Sorokin_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Sorokin_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Sorokin_(cropped).jpg)

Vladimir Sorokin reading in 2022 at Literaturhaus Zürich, Germany

At the beginning of the 21st century, [Victor Pelevin](/wiki/Victor_Pelevin "Victor Pelevin") and [Vladimir Sorokin](/wiki/Vladimir_Sorokin "Vladimir Sorokin") remained the leading and prolific Russian writers.[[141]](#cite_note-Aslanyan-141)[[142]](#cite_note-intelros-142) Pelevin became the most extensively translated one into English.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhagi20213-140) Also significant are the new works of [Boris Akunin](/wiki/Boris_Akunin "Boris Akunin") ([adventure fiction](/wiki/Adventure_fiction "Adventure fiction")), [Lyudmila Ulitskaya](/wiki/Lyudmila_Ulitskaya "Lyudmila Ulitskaya") (the *Daniel Stein, Interpreter*, a novel about the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") and interreligious relations), and [Mikhali Shishkin](/wiki/Mikhail_Shishkin_(writer) "Mikhail Shishkin (writer)") (the novel *Maidenhair*).[[141]](#cite_note-Aslanyan-141)[[142]](#cite_note-intelros-142)

Among the debutants in prose are Eduard Kochergin (b. 1937) with his novels *Angel's Doll* and *Baptized with Crosses*, [Alexei Ivanov](/wiki/Alexei_Ivanov_(writer) "Alexei Ivanov (writer)") (b. 1969) known for his novel *The Heart of Parma*, a Russian-Israeli writer and poet in the philosophical-symbolic vein Alexander Ilichevsky (b. 1970), who wrote *The Persian* and the *Newton's Drawing*,[[141]](#cite_note-Aslanyan-141)[[142]](#cite_note-intelros-142)[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKatsmanShrayer2023"Russian-Israeli_Prose_in_the_Second_Decade_of_the_Twenty-First_Century"-143) the author of novel *The Librarian* [Mikhail Elizarov](/wiki/Mikhail_Elizarov "Mikhail Elizarov") (b. 1973), and [German Sadulaev](/wiki/German_Sadulaev "German Sadulaev") (b. 1973) with the book *I am a Chechen!*[[141]](#cite_note-Aslanyan-141) In the second decade of the century, the following novelists gained fame: [Eugene Vodolazkin](/wiki/Eugene_Vodolazkin "Eugene Vodolazkin") (b. 1964) for *[The Laurus](/wiki/Laurus_(book) "Laurus (book)")* (one of ten best world novels about God by *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* version),[[144]](#cite_note-144) Sofia Sinitskaya (b. 1972), the author of the neutral novel *Black Siberia* on [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War"),[[145]](#cite_note-145) and Alexei Salnikov (b. 1978) for his hallucinatory *The Petrovs in and Around the Flu* (regarded as a rare outstanding text, see also the film *[Petrov's Flu](/wiki/Petrov%27s_Flu "Petrov's Flu")*).[[146]](#cite_note-146) In the form of popular fiction, [post-apocalyptic novels](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction") of [Dmitry Glukhovsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Glukhovsky "Dmitry Glukhovsky") (b. 1979) are successful.

Almost all of the authors named criticized [Putinism](/wiki/Putinism "Putinism") and have left Russia. After 2022, they have been "[canceled](/wiki/Cancel_culture "Cancel culture")" and their books have been withdrawn from a number of Russian booksellers.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148) Examples of active supporters of the political regime among eminent writers are poet [Yunna Morits](/wiki/Yunna_Morits "Yunna Morits") (b. 1937)[[149]](#cite_note-149) and nationalists [Alexander Prokhanov](/wiki/Alexander_Prokhanov "Alexander Prokhanov") (b. 1938),[[150]](#cite_note-150) Yurii Poliakov (b. 1954)[[151]](#cite_note-151) Pavel Krusanov (b. 1961)[[152]](#cite_note-152) and [Zakhar Prilepin](/wiki/Zakhar_Prilepin "Zakhar Prilepin") (b. 1975).[[141]](#cite_note-Aslanyan-141)[[153]](#cite_note-153)

A new generation of Russian authors appeared, differing greatly from the [postmodernist Russian](/wiki/Russian_postmodernism "Russian postmodernism") prose of the late 20th century, which led critics to speak about "new realism" as one of several contemporary literary trends ([Pavel Basinsky](/wiki/Pavel_Basinsky "Pavel Basinsky") (b. 1961), Aleksey Varlamov (b. 1963), [Alexei Ivanov](/wiki/Alexei_Ivanov_(writer) "Alexei Ivanov (writer)"), Andrei Rubanov (b. 1969), [Oleg Pavlov](/wiki/Oleg_Pavlov "Oleg Pavlov") (1970–2018), [Andrei Ivanov](/wiki/Andrei_Ivanov_(writer) "Andrei Ivanov (writer)") (b. 1971), [Roman Senchin](/wiki/Roman_Senchin "Roman Senchin") (b. 1971), [German Sadulaev](/wiki/German_Sadulaev "German Sadulaev"), [Zakhar Prilepin](/wiki/Zakhar_Prilepin "Zakhar Prilepin"), and others).

> among the shining/(branching) still people/  
> to create a human being while you are not  
> a human.

Nika Skandiaka, *to create that when you do not...*,[[154]](#cite_note-Kukulin-154) translated by the Wikipedia editors

The treasury of Russian poetry has been replenished with works by both senior masters, like [Oleg Chukhontsev](/wiki/Oleg_Chukhontsev "Oleg Chukhontsev") (b. 1938), and such debutants as Natalia Azarova (b. 1956), Vsevolod Emelin (b. 1959), Tatiana Grauz (b. 1964), Andrei Polyakov (b. 1968), [Andrei Sen-Senkov](/wiki/Andrei_Sen-Senkov "Andrei Sen-Senkov") (b. 1968), Tania Skarynkina (b. 1969), Igor Bulatovsky (b. 1971), Vlad Malenko (b. 1971), Andrei Rodionov (b. 1971), Anna Glazova (b. 1973), [Victor Ivaniv](/wiki/Victor_Ivaniv "Victor Ivaniv") (1977–2015), Eugenia Rits (b. 1977), Ekaterina Simonova (b. 1977), Pavel Goldin (b. 1978), Nika Skandiaka (b. 1978), Anna Zolotaryova (b. 1978), Roman Osminkin (b. 1979), Sergey Tenyatnikov (b. 1981), Vasily Borodin (1982–2021), Tatiana Moseeva (b. 1983), Alla Gorbunova (b. 1985), [Vera Polozkova](/wiki/Vera_Polozkova "Vera Polozkova") (b. 1986), Yevgenia Suslova (b. 1986), Nikita Ivanov (b. 1989), [Galina Rymbu](/wiki/Galina_Rymbu "Galina Rymbu") (b. 1990), [Daria Serenko](/wiki/Daria_Serenko "Daria Serenko") (b. 1993), and Maria Malinovskaya (b. 1994). The main trends of contemporary poetry are neo-surrealist fragmentation, as well as the return of plot poetry among representatives of the “New Epic” movement.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandler2024-139)[[154]](#cite_note-Kukulin-154) Two new literary prizes were established and became influential: the [Big Book](/wiki/Big_Book_(award) "Big Book (award)") and the [National Bestseller](/wiki/National_Bestseller_Literary_Prize "National Bestseller Literary Prize").[[142]](#cite_note-intelros-142)
